title: K12Orgs
text:

While preparing the K12Enrollment data, I already joined the organizational data and created a dataset that has all the individuals that graduated from a school located in the Northwest region.

The one metric that wasn’t included when I joined the enrollment data with the organizational data is the number of years they attended a school in the Northwest region (as defined by the project).


enrollment.original <- read_csv("/Users/kellyasche/Library/CloudStorage/GoogleDrive-kasche@ruralmn.org/My Drive/Research/FY25-27/FY26/OHE Journey to meaningful employment/Data/SLEDS/K12 Enrollment/K12Enrollment.csv")  %>%
  mutate(PersonID = as.integer(PersonID),
         K12OrganizationID = as.integer(K12OrganizationID)) %>%
  drop_na(PersonID)

k12.org.original <- read_csv("~/Library/CloudStorage/GoogleDrive-kasche@ruralmn.org/My Drive/Research/FY25-27/FY26/OHE Journey to meaningful employment/Data/SLEDS/K12 Orgs/K12Organization-updated.csv") %>%
  distinct(K12OrganizationID, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%
  drop_na(K12OrganizationID) %>%
  mutate(K12OrganizationID = as.integer(K12OrganizationID))

k12.org.master <- k12.org.original %>%
  select(K12OrganizationID, County, OrganizationName, DistrictID, SchoolNumber) %>%
  rename(county.name = 2) %>%
  mutate(county.name = str_replace(county.name, " County", "")) %>%
  select(K12OrganizationID, OrganizationName, county.name) %>%
  left_join(counties.regions.1, by = c("county.name" = "Name")) %>%
  select(-Dem_RUCA, -mif, -edr.simple, -planning.region)

enrollment.org.central <- enrollment.original %>%
  left_join(k12.org.master, by = "K12OrganizationID") %>%
  filter(project.pr == "Northwest")

n.years <- original %>%
  select(PersonID, grad.year, K12OrganizationID) %>%
  rename(grad.org.id = K12OrganizationID) %>%
  left_join(enrollment.original[,c(1,2,3,4)], by = "PersonID") %>%
  left_join(k12.org.original[,c(1,2,3,7)], by = "K12OrganizationID") %>%
  mutate(year = str_sub(TimeID, 1, 4)) %>%
  distinct(PersonID, year, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%
  group_by(PersonID) %>%
  summarize(n.years.attended = n()) %>%
  ungroup()

master <- original %>%
  left_join(n.years, by = "PersonID") %>%
  mutate(Dem_Desc = fct_relevel(Dem_Desc, "Entirely rural", "Town/rural mix", "Urban/town/rural mix", "Entirely urban"))

title: K12Orgs
text:
Summary of high school location
Lets breakdown the graduates by which regions they graduated from.
Over half of students graduated from a county labeled as town/rural mix, while 18% graduated from a urban/town/rural mix, 15% from an entirely urban county, and 10% from entirely rural.








Below is a map with the percentages of graduates graduating from high schools located within each county. The schools with the highest percent of graduates/observations are located in Clay with 15% followed by Otter Tail with 14% and Beltrami with 11%. All other counties ranged from 1.5% to 10% of the graduates.

title: K12Orgs
text:
Summary of student demographics
The following analysis looks to see if there are any statistical differences due to rural-ness and geographic location of the high school for the following graduate demographics;

Were eligible for free or reduced lunch at some point in the dataset
Are Female
Have been homeless at some point in the dataset
Had limited english proficiency at some point in the dataset
Had non english speakders in home at some point in the dataset
Participated in a PSEO course at some point in the dataset.

Lets start with the cross-tabs based on RUCA categories.
The cross-tabs below indicate that there are 4 demographic variables that have proportions statistically different across RUCA categories. Each of the variables are indicated with an asterisk.

Free or reduced lunch: a statistically significant higher percentage of graduates from high schools in entirely rural mix counties receive free or reduced lunch.
Homeless: as a school district becomes more urban, the percentage of it’s graduates indicating they experienced homeslessness at some point increases significantly.
PSEO: a siginficantly lower percentage of graduates from entirely rural districts participated in a PSEO program.


RUCA Free or Reduced LunchRUCA FemaleRUCA HomelessRUCA Limited EnglishRUCA non-english in homeRUCA PSEO


Although 35.5% of the total number of graduates in the data set receive free or reduced lunch, graduates from entirely rural schools had significantly higher percentage with 43%, and entirely urban schools had significantly lower share of graduates with 28%.


CrossTable(master$Dem_Desc, master$economic.status, expected = TRUE, prop.t = FALSE, prop.c = FALSE, prop.chisq = FALSE)


 
   Cell Contents
|-------------------------|
|                       N |
|              Expected N |
|           N / Row Total |
|-------------------------|

 
Total Observations in Table:  65650 

 
                     | master$economic.status 
     master$Dem_Desc |         0 |         1 | Row Total | 
---------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
      Entirely rural |      3614 |      2725 |      6339 | 
                     |  4085.831 |  2253.169 |           | 
                     |     0.570 |     0.430 |     0.097 | 
---------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
      Town/rural mix |     23650 |     13287 |     36937 | 
                     | 23807.908 | 13129.092 |           | 
                     |     0.640 |     0.360 |     0.563 | 
---------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
Urban/town/rural mix |      7963 |      4535 |     12498 | 
                     |  8055.642 |  4442.358 |           | 
                     |     0.637 |     0.363 |     0.190 | 
---------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
      Entirely urban |      7088 |      2788 |      9876 | 
                     |  6365.620 |  3510.380 |           | 
                     |     0.718 |     0.282 |     0.150 | 
---------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
        Column Total |     42315 |     23335 |     65650 | 
---------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|

 
Statistics for All Table Factors


Pearson's Chi-squared test 
------------------------------------------------------------
Chi^2 =  389.8668     d.f. =  3     p =  3.467391e-84 


 




k12.dem.free.lunch.ruca <- master %>%
  group_by(Dem_Desc) %>%
  summarise(n = n(),
           free.lunch = sum(economic.status)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(pct = free.lunch / n,
         total.pct = sum(free.lunch) / sum(n),
         data_id = as.character(seq(n()))) 

k12.dem.free.lunch.ruca.plot <- ggplot(k12.dem.free.lunch.ruca, aes(Dem_Desc, pct, fill = Dem_Desc)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = k12.dem.free.lunch.ruca$total.pct, color = "black") +
  geom_col_interactive(size = 5, aes(data_id = data_id, tooltip = paste(Dem_Desc, "\nTotal graduates: ", comma(n, accuracy = 1), "\nGraduates received free or reduced lunch: ", comma(free.lunch, accuracy = 1), "\nPercent of total graduates: ", percent(pct, accuracy = .1), sep = ""))) +
  annotate(geom = "text",
           x = 3,
           y = k12.dem.free.lunch.ruca$total.pct,
           label = paste("All graduates\npercent, ", percent(k12.dem.free.lunch.ruca$total.pct, accuracy = .1)),
           size = 5) +
  labs(x="", y = "", color="", fill = "", title = "Percent of graduates receiving free or reduced lunch")+
  geom_label(aes(label = percent(pct, accuracy = .1)), show.legend = FALSE, size = 5) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::percent)+
  theme_line+
  scale_fill_manual(values= color.ruca,
                     guide = guide_legend(ncol = 3)) +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        text = element_text(size = 16),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = .9))

  girafe(ggobj = k12.dem.free.lunch.ruca.plot) %>%
    girafe_options(opts_selection(type = "none"),
                   opts_toolbar(saveaspng = FALSE),
                   opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE))








No significance.

k12.dem.homeless.ruca <- master %>%
  mutate(HomelessIndicator = ifelse(HomelessIndicator == "Y", 1, 0)) %>%
  group_by(Dem_Desc) %>%
  summarise(n = n(),
           HomelessIndicator = sum(HomelessIndicator)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(pct = HomelessIndicator / n,
         total.pct = sum(HomelessIndicator) / sum(n),
         data_id = as.character(seq(n()))) 

k12.dem.homeless.ruca.plot <- ggplot(k12.dem.homeless.ruca, aes(Dem_Desc, pct, fill = Dem_Desc)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = k12.dem.homeless.ruca$total.pct, color = "black") +
  geom_col_interactive(size = 5, aes(data_id = data_id, tooltip = paste(Dem_Desc, "\nTotal graduates: ", comma(n, accuracy = 1), "\nGraduates indicating homelessness: ", comma(HomelessIndicator, accuracy = 1), "\nPercent of total graduates: ", percent(pct, accuracy = .1), sep = ""))) +
  annotate(geom = "text",
           x = 3,
           y = k12.dem.homeless.ruca$total.pct,
           label = paste("All graduates\npercent, ", percent(k12.dem.homeless.ruca$total.pct, accuracy = .1)),
           size = 5) +
  labs(x="", y = "", color="", fill = "", title = "Percent of graduates homeless")+
  geom_label(aes(label = percent(pct, accuracy = .1)), show.legend = FALSE, size = 5) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::percent)+
  theme_line+
  scale_fill_manual(values= color.ruca,
                     guide = guide_legend(ncol = 3)) +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        text = element_text(size = 16),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = .9))

  girafe(ggobj = k12.dem.homeless.ruca.plot) %>%
    girafe_options(opts_selection(type = "none"),
                   opts_toolbar(saveaspng = FALSE),
                   opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE))

Although 37.3% of all graduates participating in PSEO, there was a significantly lower percentage in entirely urban schools (20.0%).

                     | master$pseo.participant

k12.pseo.ruca<- master %>%
  drop_na(pseo.participant) %>%
  group_by(Dem_Desc) %>%
  summarise(n = n(),
            pseo.participant = sum(pseo.participant, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(pct = pseo.participant / n,
         total.pct = sum(pseo.participant) / sum(n),
         data_id = seq(n()))
  
k12.pseo.ruca.plot <- ggplot(k12.pseo.ruca, aes(Dem_Desc, pct, fill = Dem_Desc)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = k12.pseo.ruca$total.pct, color = "black") +
  geom_col_interactive(size = 5, aes(data_id = data_id, tooltip = paste(Dem_Desc, "\nTotal graduates: ", comma(n, accuracy = 1), "\nGraduates participated in PSEO: ", comma(pseo.participant, accuracy = 1), "\nPercent of total graduates: ", percent(pct, accuracy = .1), sep = ""))) +
  annotate(geom = "text",
           x = 1,
           y = k12.pseo.ruca$total.pct,
           label = paste("All graduates\npercent, ", percent(k12.pseo.ruca$total.pct, accuracy = .1)),
           size = 5) +
  labs(x="", y = "", color="", fill = "", title = "Percent of graduates participated in PSEO")+
  geom_label(aes(label = percent(pct, accuracy = .1)), show.legend = FALSE, size = 5) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::percent)+
  theme_line+
  scale_fill_manual(values= color.ruca,
                     guide = guide_legend(ncol = 3)) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        text = element_text(size = 16),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = .9))

  girafe(ggobj = k12.pseo.ruca.plot) %>%
    girafe_options(opts_selection(type = "none"),
                   opts_toolbar(saveaspng = FALSE),
                   opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE))

title: Post-secondary pathways
text:
Tier 1: Core Postsecondary Completion
These outcomes are the most foundational and are most closely tied to long-term employment and economic stability.

ps.grad (graduated from postsecondary)

Question: Who completes a postsecondary credential?
Why it matters: Credential completion is consistently the strongest predictor of sustained employment and earnings. This outcome captures whether students translate postsecondary participation into tangible labor market value.

highest.cred.level

Question: What level of credential do graduates earn?
Why it matters: Certificates, associate degrees, and bachelor’s degrees lead to very different employment opportunities and wage trajectories. This outcome distinguishes not just completion, but the depth of educational attainment.

title: Post-secondary pathways
text:
Tier 2: Secondary / Stratifying Postsecondary Outcomes
These outcomes add important context by differentiating how and where postsecondary education occurs.

ps.grad.InstitutionSector

Question: From what type of institution do students graduate?
Why it matters: Institutional sector (e.g., public two-year, public four-year, private) shapes cost, access, program offerings, and alignment with regional labor markets.

ps.grad.location (used when geography matters)

Question: Where do students complete their postsecondary education?
Why it matters: Location reveals patterns of mobility, regional retention, and place-based opportunity. It is especially relevant for understanding workforce pipelines and regional equity.

title: Post-secondary pathways
text:
Tier 3: Early Access Outcome (Optional)
This outcome captures initial engagement with postsecondary education and helps contextualize later attainment.

attended.ps (ever attended postsecondary)

Question: Who participates in postsecondary education at any point?
Why it matters: This provides a broad view of access beyond immediate enrollment, helping distinguish barriers to entry from barriers to completion.
Each dependent variable has the following categories;

data <- master.original %>%
  select(ps.grad, highest.cred.level, ps.grad.InstitutionSector, ps.grad.location, attended.ps) %>%
  mutate(across(c("ps.grad":"attended.ps"), factor))

title: Post-secondary pathways
section: Tier 1 Integrated Summary: Postsecondary Completion and Credential Level
text:
Tier 1 Integrated Summary: Postsecondary Completion and Credential Level
Tier 1 examined two core outcomes: whether students complete postsecondary education (ps.grad) and the highest credential earned (highest.cred.level). Across both outcomes, clear and consistent patterns emerge.
Strongest Predictors Across Tier 1
PSEO Participation
PSEO participation is the single strongest predictor across both completion and credential level. Students who participate in PSEO are dramatically more likely to complete postsecondary education and far more likely to earn a bachelor’s degree. Early exposure to college coursework sharply differentiates both persistence and upward attainment.
Academic Readiness (ACT and MCA Scores)
Academic performance consistently predicts both whether students graduate and how far they go. Higher ACT scores and stronger MCA proficiency in Math, Reading, and Science are strongly associated with completion. These differences become even more pronounced at the bachelor’s level, where academic readiness clearly separates bachelor’s earners from associate earners and non-completers.
Economic Status
Socioeconomic disadvantage is strongly associated with lower completion and lower credential levels. Students eligible for free or reduced-price lunch are far less likely to complete postsecondary education and substantially less likely to earn bachelor’s degrees. Economic gaps widen at higher levels of attainment.
Special Education Status
Students who received special education services are significantly less likely to complete postsecondary education and are very unlikely to earn bachelor’s degrees. The disparity intensifies when examining credential level.
Persistent but Moderately Sized Predictors
Race/Ethnicity
Racial and ethnic disparities are substantial across both outcomes. American Indian, Black, and Hispanic students are far more likely to not graduate, while White and Asian/PI students are more likely to earn bachelor’s degrees. Inequities are particularly visible at higher levels of attainment.
Gender
Women are more likely than men to complete postsecondary education and to earn bachelor’s degrees. The gender gap is most pronounced at the bachelor’s level rather than at lower credential tiers.
CTE Measures
CTE participation and course-taking patterns are associated with different credential pathways. Higher CTE course volume aligns more closely with associate-level attainment, while bachelor’s attainment is associated with fewer CTE courses. These relationships are meaningful but not as strong as academic readiness or PSEO participation.
Weak Contextual Predictors
Geographic and Economic Context (EDR, Rural-ness, County Unemployment, County Wages)
High school contextual characteristics are consistently weak predictors across both completion and credential level. While regional differences are statistically detectable, geographic and local economic conditions explain only a small portion of variation in attainment.

Overall Tier 1 Pattern
Across both completion and credential level, the dominant drivers of postsecondary success in Northwest Minnesota are academic preparation and early college exposure, followed closely by structural demographic inequities. Geographic and local economic context play a comparatively minor role.
Importantly, disparities intensify at higher levels of attainment. Predictors that influence completion become even more pronounced when distinguishing bachelor’s degree earners from all other groups. Tier 1 therefore suggests that improving academic readiness and expanding meaningful early college opportunities are central levers for increasing both postsecondary completion and higher-level credential attainment in the region.

title: Post-secondary pathways
text:
ps.grad

Demographics
1. Race/Ethnicity (Cramér’s V = 0.298)
Race and ethnicity show the strongest relationship with postsecondary graduation. Completion rates vary widely across groups, with White students having the highest likelihood of graduating and substantially lower completion among American Indian, Black, and Hispanic students. American Indian students are particularly overrepresented among non-completers, indicating pronounced racial and ethnic inequities in postsecondary attainment.
2. Economic Status (φ = 0.259)
Economic disadvantage is strongly associated with lower postsecondary graduation rates. Students eligible for free or reduced-price lunch are far less likely to complete a credential and are disproportionately represented among non-completers, highlighting persistent socioeconomic barriers to postsecondary success.
3. Special Education Status (φ = 0.254)
Special education status is also strongly related to postsecondary graduation. Students who required special education services complete postsecondary credentials at much lower rates than their peers, suggesting substantial structural and support-related challenges in translating secondary education into postsecondary attainment.
4. Gender (φ = 0.122)
Gender has the weakest relationship among the demographic variables examined, though it remains meaningful. Women are more likely than men to complete a postsecondary credential, while men are disproportionately represented among non-completers. Compared to other demographic factors, however, the gender gap is more modest.

Gendereconomic.statusRaceEthnicitySpecialEdStatus


Statistical relationship?: Yes
Strength of relationship: Small-to-moderate (φ = 0.122, p < .001)
Description of relationship: Women are more likely than men to complete a postsecondary credential. Just over half of women (50.3%) graduated from postsecondary education, compared with 38.1% of men. Men are disproportionately represented among non-completers, indicating a meaningful gender gap in postsecondary completion in Northwest Minnesota.

High School Characteristics
1. Economic Development Region (Cramér’s V = 0.086)
Economic Development Region shows the strongest relationship among the high school characteristics, though the effect remains small. Students from EDR 4 – West Central have the highest completion rate (47.2%), followed by EDR 1 – Northwest (43.0%), while EDR 2 – Headwaters has the lowest rate (36.0%). Regional context appears to matter, but only modestly.
2. Rural-ness of School District (Dem_Desc) (Cramér’s V = 0.030)
District rural classification is statistically related to postsecondary completion, but the effect is very small. Completion rates vary only slightly across district types, ranging from about 41% to 47%. Rural-ness alone does little to meaningfully explain differences in postsecondary graduation.
3. Average County Unemployment Rate (ANOVA, p < .001)
Average unemployment during high school years differs statistically between completers and non-completers, but the mean difference is very small (≈ 0.13 percentage points). Local unemployment conditions during high school have minimal practical association with postsecondary completion.
4. Average County Wages as Percent of State (ANOVA, p < .001)
Average county wage levels during high school years are statistically associated with completion, but the difference is extremely small (≈ 0.01). County-level wage context provides very limited explanatory value for postsecondary graduation outcomes.
Overall Pattern:
Compared to demographic factors, high school contextual characteristics are far weaker predictors of postsecondary completion. While region shows a modest relationship, broader geographic and economic context variables explain only a small portion of variation in who completes a credential in Northwest Minnesota.

Description of relationship: Average county wages (as a percent of the Minnesota average) during high school years differ statistically between completers and non-completers. Students who completed postsecondary education attended high schools in counties with slightly higher relative wages (mean difference ≈ 0.01). However, the magnitude of this difference is extremely small, suggesting that county-level wage context during high school has minimal practical influence on postsecondary completion.

High School Accomplishments
1. PSEO Participation (φ = 0.299)
Participation in postsecondary educational opportunities during high school shows the strongest relationship with postsecondary completion. Students who participated in PSEO graduate at dramatically higher rates (63.1%) than non-participants (31.7%). Early exposure to college-level coursework appears to be one of the most powerful predictors of eventual credential completion.
2. ACT Composite Score (ANOVA, p < .001; mean difference ≈ 2.78 points)
ACT performance is strongly associated with postsecondary graduation. Students who completed a credential scored nearly three points higher on average than non-completers, indicating that academic preparation and college readiness play a major role in completion outcomes.
3. MCA Reading (ANOVA, p < .001; mean difference ≈ 0.51)
Reading proficiency shows a substantial relationship with completion. Students who graduated from postsecondary programs scored meaningfully higher in reading than non-completers, underscoring the importance of literacy skills for long-term academic persistence.
4. MCA Math (ANOVA, p < .001; mean difference ≈ 0.49)
Math proficiency is also strongly associated with completion, with completers scoring nearly half a point higher on average. Academic skill gaps in mathematics appear closely tied to postsecondary success.
5. MCA Science (ANOVA, p < .001; mean difference ≈ 0.44)
Science proficiency follows a similar pattern, with completers scoring moderately higher than non-completers. While slightly smaller than math and reading gaps, the difference remains meaningful.
6. CTE Achievement Status (Cramér’s V = 0.085)
CTE achievement category shows a small relationship with postsecondary graduation. Students with no CTE participation have the highest completion rates, while concentrators/completers have somewhat lower rates. The differences are statistically meaningful but modest in magnitude.
7. Total CTE Courses Taken (ANOVA, p < .001; mean difference ≈ -0.70)
The total number of CTE courses taken has a small relationship with completion. Postsecondary completers took slightly fewer CTE courses on average than non-completers. Although statistically significant, the practical difference is limited.
Overall Pattern:
High school accomplishments—particularly indicators of academic readiness and early college exposure—are substantially stronger predictors of postsecondary completion than high school contextual characteristics. PSEO participation and academic performance measures (ACT and MCA scores) stand out as the most influential factors, while CTE-related measures show weaker, though still statistically meaningful, associations.

Summary of ps.grad
Strong and Consistent Predictors
PSEO Participation (φ = 0.299)
Early exposure to college coursework is one of the strongest predictors of completion. Students who participated in PSEO graduate at dramatically higher rates than non-participants, indicating that structured early access to postsecondary experiences substantially increases the likelihood of earning a credential.
Academic Performance (ACT and MCA Scores)
ACT Composite scores and MCA proficiency in Math, Reading, and Science all show moderate relationships with completion. Students who complete postsecondary education consistently demonstrate higher academic proficiency in high school. Academic readiness is a central driver of postsecondary success.
Race/Ethnicity (Cramér’s V = 0.298)
Racial and ethnic disparities in completion are pronounced. White students complete at substantially higher rates than several other groups, particularly American Indian students. These inequities are large and persistent.
Economic Status (φ = 0.259)
Socioeconomic disadvantage is strongly associated with lower completion. Students eligible for free or reduced-price lunch graduate at far lower rates, underscoring the structural impact of economic barriers.
Moderate Predictors
Special Education Status (φ = 0.254)
Students who required special education services complete at significantly lower rates, suggesting challenges in transitioning from secondary to postsecondary systems.
CTE Measures (Cramér’s V = 0.085; ANOVA p < .001)
CTE achievement status and total CTE courses taken show small but meaningful relationships with completion. However, their predictive strength is notably weaker than academic readiness and PSEO participation.
Weak Contextual Predictors
Economic Development Region (Cramér’s V = 0.086)
Regional differences exist, but the effect size is small. Region alone explains only a modest share of variation in completion.
Rural-ness of District (Cramér’s V = 0.030)
District rural classification has a statistically detectable but practically minimal relationship with completion.
County Unemployment and Wage Context (ANOVA p < .001)
Although statistically significant, differences in local economic conditions during high school are extremely small in magnitude and provide little explanatory value for postsecondary graduation.
Overall Pattern
Postsecondary completion in Northwest Minnesota is driven far more by individual academic readiness and early college exposure than by geographic or local economic context. Demographic inequities—particularly by race/ethnicity and economic status—remain substantial. High school accomplishments, especially PSEO participation and academic proficiency, emerge as the most influential levers associated with whether students ultimately earn a credential.

title: Post-secondary pathways
text:
highest.cred.level

Demographics
1. Economic Status (Cramér’s V = 0.272)
Economic status shows the strongest relationship with highest credential earned. Students not eligible for free or reduced-price lunch are far more likely to earn a bachelor’s degree or higher and far less likely to leave postsecondary without graduating. Economically disadvantaged students are disproportionately concentrated among non-completers, indicating that socioeconomic status strongly shapes both completion and credential level.
2. Special Education Status (Cramér’s V = 0.266)
Special education status is nearly as strong a predictor as economic status. Students who received special education services are overwhelmingly more likely to not graduate from postsecondary education and very unlikely to earn a bachelor’s degree. The disparities extend beyond completion into the level of attainment.
3. Race/Ethnicity (Cramér’s V = 0.216)
Race and ethnicity show a substantial relationship with credential level. White and Asian/PI students are more likely to earn bachelor’s degrees, while American Indian, Black, and Hispanic students are far more likely to not graduate. These differences reflect persistent inequities in both access to and attainment of higher-level credentials.
4. Gender (Cramér’s V = 0.141)
Gender has the weakest relationship among the demographic variables, though it remains meaningful. Women are more likely than men to earn a bachelor’s degree and less likely to leave postsecondary without graduating. The gender gap is most pronounced at the bachelor’s level rather than at the associate degree level.
Overall Pattern:
Demographic factors are strongly associated not only with whether students complete postsecondary education, but also with the level of credential earned. Socioeconomic status and special education status emerge as the most influential demographic predictors of credential level, followed by race/ethnicity. Gender differences are present but comparatively smaller. Across all variables, disparities widen at the bachelor’s degree level, indicating that equity gaps are particularly pronounced at higher levels of attainment.

Women are more likely than men to earn a bachelor’s degree or higher (33.6% vs. 21.5%) and less likely to leave postsecondary without graduating (49.9% vs. 62.0%). Rates of earning an associate degree or lesser credential are nearly identical across genders (16.5%). The primary gender gap appears at the bachelor’s degree level and in overall completion.

title: Post-secondary pathways
text:
High School Characteristics
1. Economic Development Region (Cramér’s V = 0.066)
Economic Development Region shows the strongest relationship among high school contextual variables, though the effect remains small. Students from EDR 4 – West Central have the highest bachelor’s degree attainment and lowest non-completion rates, while EDR 2 – Headwaters has the lowest bachelor’s attainment and highest non-completion rates. Regional context appears to matter modestly for credential level.
2. Rural-ness of School District (Dem_Desc) (Cramér’s V = 0.040)
District rural classification is statistically associated with credential level, but the magnitude is very small. Bachelor’s degree attainment is somewhat higher in entirely urban districts, while non-completion rates are slightly higher in mixed and rural districts. Differences across district types are limited in size.
3. Average County Unemployment Rate (ANOVA, p < .001)
County unemployment during high school years differs slightly across credential levels, but differences are minimal. Non-completers attended high schools in counties with marginally lower unemployment rates, and bachelor’s and associate degree earners do not meaningfully differ from one another. The practical influence is negligible.
4. Average County Wages as Percent of State (ANOVA, p < .001)
County wage context is statistically related to credential level, but differences are extremely small. Associate degree earners attended high schools in counties with slightly higher relative wages, while non-completers attended counties with the lowest relative wages. The magnitude of these differences provides little substantive explanatory power.
Overall Pattern:
High school contextual characteristics show weak relationships with highest credential earned. While regional differences are present, geographic and economic context explain only a small portion of variation in credential level. Compared to demographic and academic accomplishment variables, high school characteristics play a limited role in shaping postsecondary attainment levels in Northwest Minnesota.

title: Post-secondary pathways
text:
High School Accomplishments
1. PSEO Participation (Cramér’s V = 0.333)
PSEO participation shows the strongest relationship with credential level. Students who participated in PSEO are dramatically more likely to earn a bachelor’s degree or higher and far less likely to leave postsecondary without graduating. Early college exposure sharply differentiates not just completion, but upward movement into higher-level credentials.
2. ACT Composite Score (ANOVA, p < .001; large mean differences)
ACT scores strongly distinguish bachelor’s degree earners from both associate degree earners and non-completers. Bachelor’s earners scored nearly four points higher than non-completers. Academic readiness, as measured by ACT performance, is a powerful predictor of attaining a bachelor’s degree.
3. MCA Proficiency (Math, Reading, Science) (ANOVA, p < .001)
MCA Math, Reading, and Science scores all show strong relationships with credential level. Bachelor’s degree earners consistently outperform associate degree earners, who in turn outperform non-completers. Academic proficiency clearly differentiates levels of attainment, especially at the bachelor’s level.
4. Total CTE Courses Taken (ANOVA, p < .001)
The number of CTE courses taken shows a moderate relationship with credential level. Students earning associate degrees take the most CTE courses on average, while bachelor’s degree earners take substantially fewer. CTE course volume appears aligned with associate-level pathways rather than bachelor’s attainment.
5. CTE Achievement Status (Cramér’s V = 0.115)
CTE achievement category shows a small-to-moderate relationship with credential level. Students without CTE participation are most likely to earn bachelor’s degrees, while concentrators/completers are more likely to earn associate degrees or not complete. CTE pathways appear associated with different postsecondary trajectories.

Overall Pattern:
High school accomplishments are strongly associated with not only whether students complete postsecondary education, but the level of credential they earn. Academic readiness indicators (PSEO participation, ACT scores, MCA proficiency) are the most powerful differentiators of bachelor’s degree attainment. CTE measures show meaningful but more pathway-specific relationships, particularly aligning with associate-level credentials. Overall, academic preparation and early exposure to college-level coursework emerge as the clearest drivers of higher-level postsecondary attainment in Northwest Minnesota.

Students who earned a bachelor’s degree or higher scored nearly 3.8 points higher on average than those earning an associate degree or lesser credential, and nearly 4 points higher than those who did not graduate. Differences between associate degree earners and non-completers are minimal and not statistically significant. Academic readiness, as measured by ACT performance, is strongly associated with earning a bachelor’s degree and clearly differentiates higher levels of attainment.

Summary of highest.cred.level
Strong and Consistent Predictors
PSEO Participation (Cramér’s V = 0.333)
Early college participation is the strongest predictor of credential level. Students who participated in PSEO are far more likely to earn a bachelor’s degree and far less likely to leave postsecondary without graduating. Early exposure to college coursework strongly differentiates higher-level attainment.
Academic Readiness (ACT and MCA Scores)
ACT Composite scores and MCA proficiency in Math, Reading, and Science all show strong relationships with credential level. Bachelor’s degree earners consistently outperform associate degree earners, who in turn outperform non-completers. Academic preparation clearly separates levels of postsecondary attainment, particularly at the bachelor’s level.
Economic Status (Cramér’s V = 0.272)
Socioeconomic status strongly shapes credential level. Students not eligible for free or reduced-price lunch are substantially more likely to earn bachelor’s degrees and far less likely to not graduate. Economic disadvantage is closely tied to lower levels of attainment.
Special Education Status (Cramér’s V = 0.266)
Students who required special education services are overwhelmingly more likely to not graduate and very unlikely to earn bachelor’s degrees. Special education status remains a major differentiator of credential level.
Moderate Predictors
Race/Ethnicity (Cramér’s V = 0.216)
Racial and ethnic disparities persist across credential levels. White and Asian/PI students are more likely to earn bachelor’s degrees, while American Indian, Black, and Hispanic students are more likely to not graduate. Gaps widen at higher levels of attainment.
Gender (Cramér’s V = 0.141)
Women are more likely than men to earn bachelor’s degrees and less likely to leave postsecondary without graduating. The gender gap is most pronounced at the bachelor’s level.
Total CTE Courses Taken (ANOVA, p < .001)
CTE course volume differentiates credential pathways. Associate degree earners take the most CTE courses, while bachelor’s degree earners take substantially fewer, suggesting pathway specialization.
CTE Achievement Status (Cramér’s V = 0.115)
CTE category is associated with different postsecondary trajectories, with concentrators/completers more likely to earn associate-level credentials and less likely to earn bachelor’s degrees.
Weak Contextual Predictors
Economic Development Region (Cramér’s V = 0.066)
Regional differences in credential level exist but are modest. Region explains only a small share of variation in attainment.
Rural-ness of District (Cramér’s V = 0.040)
District rural classification has a very small relationship with credential level.
County Unemployment and Wage Context (ANOVA, p < .001)
Local economic conditions during high school are statistically related to credential level but the differences are extremely small and offer little practical explanatory value.
Overall Pattern
Highest credential earned in Northwest Minnesota is driven primarily by academic readiness and early college exposure, with substantial demographic inequities shaping attainment levels. Academic performance and PSEO participation sharply differentiate bachelor’s degree earners from other groups. Geographic and local economic context, while statistically significant, play a comparatively minor role in explaining variation in credential level.

title: Post-secondary pathways
section: Tier 2 Integrated Summary: Institutional Sector and Graduation Location
text:
Tier 2 Integrated Summary: Institutional Sector and Graduation Location
Tier 2 examined two stratifying outcomes: the type of institution from which students graduate (ps.grad.InstitutionSector) and where they graduate (ps.grad.location). Together, these outcomes show how postsecondary pathways differentiate not just completion, but tier of institution and geographic mobility.
Strongest Predictors Across Tier 2
PSEO Participation
PSEO participation is the strongest and most consistent predictor across both institutional sector and graduation location. Students who participate in PSEO are dramatically more likely to graduate from 4-year public institutions and far less likely to not graduate. They are also substantially more likely to graduate outside the region. Early college exposure functions as a structural accelerator into bachelor’s-oriented and more mobile pathways.
Academic Readiness (ACT and MCA Scores)
Academic performance strongly differentiates both sector and location. Students with higher ACT scores and stronger MCA proficiency are far more likely to graduate from 4-year public institutions and more likely to graduate outside the region. Non-completers consistently show the lowest academic performance. Academic readiness is the primary sorting mechanism shaping both institutional tier and geographic mobility.
Economic Status
Socioeconomic status strongly shapes Tier 2 outcomes. Students not eligible for free or reduced-price lunch are far more likely to graduate from 4-year institutions and far more likely to graduate beyond the region. Economically disadvantaged students are disproportionately concentrated among non-completers and less mobile graduates. Socioeconomic disparities influence both attainment level and geographic reach.
Special Education Status
Students who received special education services are overwhelmingly more likely to not graduate and are far less likely to access 4-year or out-of-region pathways. Disparities are substantial across both institutional tier and graduation location.
Persistent but Moderately Sized Predictors
Race/Ethnicity
Racial and ethnic disparities remain evident across sector and location. White and Asian/PI students are more likely to graduate from 4-year institutions and to graduate outside the region, while American Indian, Black, and Hispanic students are more concentrated among non-completers. Disparities extend beyond completion into institutional stratification and mobility.
Gender
Women are more likely than men to graduate from 4-year institutions and more likely to graduate within or beyond Minnesota. Men are more concentrated among non-completers. Gender differences are meaningful but smaller than socioeconomic or special education disparities.
CTE Measures
CTE achievement and course volume align more strongly with 2-year public institutional pathways and somewhat with regional retention. Students with higher CTE intensity are more likely to graduate from 2-year institutions and less likely to graduate from 4-year institutions or outside the region. These patterns are meaningful but not as strong as academic readiness or PSEO participation.
Weak Contextual Predictors
Geographic and Economic Context (EDR, Rural-ness, County Unemployment, County Wages)
High school contextual characteristics are consistently weak predictors across both institutional sector and graduation location. While regional differences are statistically detectable, geographic classification and county-level economic conditions explain only a small share of variation in postsecondary stratification and mobility.

Overall Tier 2 Pattern
Across both institutional sector and graduation location, the dominant drivers of stratification in Northwest Minnesota are academic preparation and early college exposure, followed closely by structural demographic inequities.
Students with strong academic readiness and access to PSEO are far more likely to enter 4-year public institutions and to graduate beyond the region. Students facing socioeconomic disadvantage or special education barriers are disproportionately concentrated in non-completion and lower-tier or less mobile pathways. High school context contributes comparatively little explanatory power.
Tier 2 shows that postsecondary opportunity is not only about completion—it is also about institutional tier and geographic mobility, and those pathways are structured by readiness and equity gaps.

title: Post-secondary pathways
text:
ps.grad.InstitutionSector

Demographics
1. Economic Status (Cramér’s V = 0.271)
Economic status shows the strongest relationship with institution sector. Students not eligible for free or reduced-price lunch are substantially more likely to graduate from 4-year public institutions and other college types, while economically disadvantaged students are overwhelmingly more likely to not graduate at all. Socioeconomic disparities are especially pronounced in 4-year public pathways.
2. Special Education Status (Cramér’s V = 0.267)
Special education status is nearly as strong a predictor as economic status. Students who received special education services are dramatically less likely to graduate from 4-year public institutions and far more likely to not complete postsecondary education. Disparities are particularly severe at the 4-year level.
3. Race/Ethnicity (Cramér’s V = 0.176)
Race and ethnicity show a moderate relationship with institution sector. White and Asian/PI students are more likely to graduate from 4-year public institutions, while American Indian, Black, and Hispanic students are more likely to not graduate. Sector differences reflect broader completion disparities and uneven access to bachelor’s-level pathways.
4. Gender (Cramér’s V = 0.149)
Gender shows a small-to-moderate relationship with institution sector. Women are more likely than men to graduate from 4-year public institutions and other college types, while men are more likely to not complete postsecondary education. Differences are less pronounced in 2-year public institutions.
Overall Pattern:
Demographic disparities are strongly reflected in postsecondary institution sector outcomes. Socioeconomic status and special education status are the most powerful demographic predictors of sector-level pathways, particularly in access to and completion of 4-year public institutions. Racial/ethnic and gender differences are also meaningful, with disparities most visible in bachelor’s-level institutional sectors. Across variables, inequities in sector pathways largely mirror broader patterns of postsecondary completion and attainment.

Graduation rates from 2-year public institutions are similar across genders. The primary gender differences emerge in bachelor’s-level pathways and overall completion.

title: Post-secondary pathways
text:
High School Characteristics
1. Economic Development Region (Cramér’s V = 0.072)
Economic Development Region shows the strongest relationship among high school contextual variables, though the effect remains small. EDR 4 – West Central has the highest representation in 4-year public pathways and the lowest non-completion rate, while EDR 2 – Headwaters shows the highest non-completion rate and lower 4-year participation. Regional differences are present but modest in magnitude.
2. Rural-ness of School District (Dem_Desc) (Cramér’s V = 0.037)
District rural classification is statistically associated with institution sector, but the relationship is very small. Entirely urban districts show slightly higher 4-year public graduation rates, while non-completion rates are relatively similar across district types. Rural-ness alone explains little variation in sector outcomes.
3. Average County Unemployment Rate (ANOVA, p < .001)
County unemployment during high school years differs slightly across institutional sectors, but differences are minimal. Non-completers attended high schools in counties with marginally lower unemployment rates, and there are no meaningful differences among 2-year, 4-year, and other graduates. Practical explanatory value is negligible.
4. Average County Wages as Percent of State (ANOVA, p < .001)
County wage context is statistically related to institution sector, but the magnitude is extremely small. Students graduating from 2-year public institutions attended counties with slightly higher relative wages, while non-completers attended counties with slightly lower wages. These differences provide very limited insight into sector pathways.
Overall Pattern:
High school contextual characteristics are weak predictors of postsecondary institution sector. While regional differences are modestly detectable, rural classification and county-level economic conditions explain very little variation in institutional pathways. Compared to demographic and academic accomplishment variables, high school characteristics play a limited role in shaping where students ultimately graduate.

title: Post-secondary pathways
text:
High School Accomplishments
1. PSEO Participation (Cramér’s V = 0.332)
PSEO participation is the strongest predictor of institutional sector. Students who participated in PSEO are dramatically more likely to graduate from 4-year public institutions and other college types, and far less likely to not complete postsecondary education. Early college exposure sharply differentiates 4-year pathways from 2-year and non-completion outcomes.
2. ACT Composite Score (ANOVA, p < .001; large mean differences)
ACT scores strongly distinguish sector pathways. Students graduating from 4-year public and other institutions score nearly four points higher on average than 2-year public graduates and non-completers. Academic readiness clearly separates 4-year institutional trajectories from 2-year and non-completion outcomes.
3. MCA Proficiency (Math, Reading, Science) (ANOVA, p < .001)
MCA scores in Math, Reading, and Science all show strong relationships with institution sector. Students graduating from 4-year public institutions consistently outperform 2-year graduates and non-completers. Academic proficiency strongly differentiates bachelor’s-oriented institutional pathways from others.
4. Total CTE Courses Taken (ANOVA, p < .001)
CTE course volume shows a moderate relationship with sector. Students graduating from 2-year public institutions take the most CTE courses on average, while 4-year public graduates take substantially fewer. CTE intensity aligns more closely with 2-year pathways.
5. CTE Achievement Status (Cramér’s V = 0.119)
CTE achievement category is meaningfully associated with sector. CTE concentrators/completers are more likely to graduate from 2-year public institutions, while students with no CTE participation are more likely to graduate from 4-year institutions. CTE pathways appear aligned with specific postsecondary sectors rather than uniformly predictive of completion.

Overall Pattern:
High school accomplishments—particularly academic readiness and PSEO participation—are the strongest predictors of postsecondary institution sector. Academic performance sharply differentiates 4-year public pathways from 2-year and non-completion outcomes. CTE measures align more closely with 2-year institutional trajectories. Compared to high school contextual factors, academic preparation and early college exposure play a far more decisive role in shaping where students ultimately graduate.

Description of relationship: ACT Composite scores differ substantially by postsecondary institution sector. Students who graduated from 4-year public institutions scored nearly 3.8 points higher on average than 2-year public graduates and nearly 4 points higher than non-completers. Students graduating from other types of colleges show similarly high ACT scores. There is no meaningful difference between 2-year public graduates and non-completers. Academic readiness strongly differentiates 4-year institutional pathways from 2-year and non-completion outcomes.

Summary
The previous section examines where students graduate—2-year public, 4-year public, other institutions, or not at all. Across groups of predictors, clear patterns emerge.
Strong and Consistent Predictors
PSEO Participation (Cramér’s V = 0.332)
PSEO participation is the strongest predictor of institutional sector. Students who participated in PSEO are dramatically more likely to graduate from 4-year public institutions and far less likely to not complete postsecondary education. Early college exposure sharply differentiates bachelor’s-oriented pathways from 2-year and non-completion outcomes.
Academic Readiness (ACT and MCA Scores)
ACT Composite scores and MCA proficiency in Math, Reading, and Science strongly differentiate sector pathways. Students graduating from 4-year public institutions consistently outperform 2-year public graduates and non-completers. Academic preparation clearly separates 4-year institutional trajectories from other outcomes.
Economic Status (Cramér’s V = 0.271)
Socioeconomic status strongly shapes institutional sector. Students not eligible for free or reduced-price lunch are far more likely to graduate from 4-year public institutions, while economically disadvantaged students are disproportionately represented among non-completers. Disparities are especially visible in access to 4-year public pathways.
Special Education Status (Cramér’s V = 0.267)
Students who received special education services are dramatically less likely to graduate from 4-year institutions and overwhelmingly more likely to not complete postsecondary education. Sector-level disparities mirror and intensify completion gaps.
Moderate Predictors
Race/Ethnicity (Cramér’s V = 0.176)
Racial and ethnic disparities extend into sector pathways. White and Asian/PI students are more likely to graduate from 4-year public institutions, while American Indian, Black, and Hispanic students are more likely to not graduate. Sector differences reflect broader inequities in postsecondary attainment.
Gender (Cramér’s V = 0.149)
Women are more likely than men to graduate from 4-year public institutions and less likely to not complete postsecondary education. Differences are less pronounced in 2-year public pathways.
CTE Measures (Cramér’s V = 0.119; ANOVA p < .001)
CTE achievement and course volume are associated with sector alignment. Students with higher CTE participation are more likely to graduate from 2-year public institutions, while 4-year public graduates take fewer CTE courses on average. CTE pathways appear aligned with 2-year institutional trajectories.
Weak Contextual Predictors
Economic Development Region (Cramér’s V = 0.072)
Regional differences in sector outcomes are statistically detectable but modest. Region explains only a small share of variation in institutional pathways.
Rural-ness of District (Cramér’s V = 0.037)
District rural classification has a very small relationship with institutional sector.
County Unemployment and Wage Context (ANOVA, p < .001)
Local economic conditions during high school are statistically related to institutional sector, but differences are extremely small and offer little practical explanatory value.
Overall Pattern
Postsecondary institution sector in Northwest Minnesota is shaped primarily by academic readiness, early college exposure, and structural demographic inequities. Academic performance and PSEO participation sharply differentiate 4-year public pathways from 2-year and non-completion outcomes. Socioeconomic and special education disparities remain substantial, particularly in access to and completion of 4-year institutions. In contrast, high school contextual characteristics play only a minor role in determining institutional sector outcomes.

title: Post-secondary pathways
text:
ps.grad.location

Demographics
1. Economic Status (Cramér’s V = 0.266)
Economic status shows the strongest relationship with graduation location. Students not eligible for free or reduced-price lunch are substantially more likely to graduate within the region, elsewhere in Minnesota, and outside Minnesota, while economically disadvantaged students are overwhelmingly more likely to not graduate at all. Socioeconomic disparities are evident in both completion and geographic mobility.
2. Special Education Status (Cramér’s V = 0.255)
Special education status is also a strong predictor of graduation location. Students who received special education services are far more likely to not graduate and far less likely to complete postsecondary education in any geographic location. Disparities extend beyond completion into regional retention and mobility.
3. Race/Ethnicity (Cramér’s V = 0.173)
Race and ethnicity show a moderate relationship with graduation location. White students are more likely to graduate within the region or elsewhere in Minnesota, while American Indian, Black, and Hispanic students are more likely to not graduate. Asian/PI students show relatively higher rates of graduation outside Minnesota. These patterns indicate that racial disparities shape both attainment and mobility outcomes.
4. Gender (Cramér’s V = 0.125)
Gender has the weakest relationship among the demographic variables, though it remains meaningful. Women are more likely than men to graduate within the region and elsewhere in Minnesota and are less likely to not graduate. Differences in out-of-state graduation are smaller. The primary gender gap appears in regional retention and overall completion.
Overall Pattern:
Demographic disparities strongly influence postsecondary graduation location. Socioeconomic status and special education status are the most powerful predictors, shaping not only whether students complete postsecondary education but also where they do so. Racial and gender differences are also meaningful, particularly in regional retention patterns. Across variables, inequities in geographic mobility largely mirror broader disparities in postsecondary completion.

title: Post-secondary pathways
text:
High School Characteristics
1. Economic Development Region (Cramér’s V = 0.076)
Economic Development Region shows the strongest relationship among high school contextual variables, though the effect remains small. EDR 1 – Northwest and EDR 4 – West Central have similar rates of regional graduation, while EDR 2 – Headwaters shows lower regional graduation and higher non-completion. Graduation outside Minnesota is somewhat more common among students from EDR 4. Regional differences are present but modest.
2. Rural-ness of School District (Dem_Desc) (Cramér’s V = 0.053)
District rural classification is statistically associated with graduation location, but the relationship is small. Entirely urban districts show slightly higher rates of graduation outside Minnesota, while town/rural mix districts show somewhat higher graduation within Minnesota. Differences across district types are limited in magnitude.
3. Average County Unemployment Rate (ANOVA, p < .001)
County unemployment during high school years differs modestly across graduation locations. Students graduating outside Minnesota and non-completers attended high schools in counties with slightly lower unemployment rates than regional graduates. Differences between regional graduates and those graduating elsewhere within Minnesota are negligible.
4. Average County Wages as Percent of State (ANOVA, p < .001)
County wage context is statistically related to graduation location, but the differences are extremely small. Students graduating outside Minnesota attended counties with marginally higher relative wages, while non-completers attended counties with slightly lower wages. Practical explanatory value is minimal.
Overall Pattern:
High school contextual characteristics are weak predictors of postsecondary graduation location. While regional differences are statistically detectable, rural classification and county-level economic conditions explain only a small share of variation in geographic graduation outcomes. Compared to demographic and academic accomplishment variables, high school characteristics play a limited role in determining where students ultimately graduate.

Regional graduation rates are fairly similar across district types, and non-completion rates remain consistent. Overall, district rural classification plays only a modest role in explaining where students graduate.

title: Post-secondary pathways
text:
High School Accomplishments
1. PSEO Participation (Cramér’s V = 0.304)
PSEO participation is the strongest predictor of graduation location. Students who participated in PSEO are substantially more likely to graduate within the region, elsewhere in Minnesota, and outside Minnesota, and far less likely to not graduate at all. Early college exposure strongly differentiates both completion and geographic mobility.
2. ACT Composite Score (ANOVA, p < .001; large mean differences)
ACT scores strongly distinguish graduation locations. Students graduating elsewhere in Minnesota score over 2.4 points higher than regional graduates, and those graduating outside Minnesota also score substantially higher. Non-completers score dramatically lower than all graduate groups. Academic readiness is closely tied to both completion and out-of-region mobility.
3. MCA Proficiency (Math, Reading, Science) (ANOVA, p < .001)
MCA scores in Math, Reading, and Science show strong relationships with graduation location. Students graduating outside the region consistently outperform regional graduates, and non-completers score substantially lower than all graduate groups. Academic proficiency differentiates not only completion, but patterns of geographic mobility.
4. Total CTE Courses Taken (ANOVA, p < .001)
CTE course volume shows a moderate relationship with graduation location. Students graduating elsewhere in Minnesota or outside Minnesota take fewer CTE courses than regional graduates, while non-completers take the most. Higher CTE intensity is more closely aligned with regional retention or non-completion, while lower CTE participation is associated with mobility.
5. CTE Achievement Status (Cramér’s V = 0.077)
CTE achievement status has a small relationship with graduation location. Differences across CTE groups are modest, with slight variations in regional retention and mobility. Compared to academic readiness measures, CTE pathways play a much smaller role in explaining geographic graduation outcomes.

Overall Pattern:
High school accomplishments—especially academic readiness and PSEO participation—are the strongest predictors of postsecondary graduation location. Students with higher academic performance are not only more likely to complete postsecondary education, but also more likely to graduate outside the region. CTE measures show moderate alignment with regional retention patterns, but their influence is notably weaker than academic indicators. Compared to high school contextual characteristics, academic preparation plays a far more decisive role in shaping geographic mobility of postsecondary attainment.

ACTCompositeScorepseo.participantcte.achievementtotal.cte.courses.takenMCA.MMCA.RMCA.S

Strength of relationship: Strong (ANOVA, p < .001)
Description of relationship: ACT Composite scores differ substantially by postsecondary graduation location. Students who graduated elsewhere in Minnesota scored about 2.46 points higher than regional graduates, while those graduating outside Minnesota scored about 1.87 points higher than regional graduates. Non-completers scored significantly lower than all graduate groups—over 4 points lower than those graduating elsewhere in Minnesota. Academic readiness strongly differentiates not only completion, but geographic mobility of postsecondary attainment.

Summary
Tier 2 (Location) examines where students graduate—within the region, elsewhere in Minnesota, outside Minnesota, or not at all. Across predictor groups, clear themes emerge.
Strong and Consistent Predictors
PSEO Participation (Cramér’s V = 0.304)
PSEO participation is one of the strongest predictors of graduation location. Participants are far more likely to graduate in any location and far less likely to not graduate at all. Early college exposure strongly differentiates both completion and geographic mobility.
Academic Readiness (ACT and MCA Scores)
ACT Composite scores and MCA proficiency in Math, Reading, and Science strongly differentiate graduation locations. Students graduating outside the region or elsewhere in Minnesota consistently score higher than regional graduates, while non-completers score substantially lower than all graduate groups. Academic preparation is tightly linked not only to completion, but to geographic mobility.
Economic Status (Cramér’s V = 0.266)
Socioeconomic status strongly shapes graduation location. Students not eligible for free or reduced-price lunch are far more likely to graduate within or beyond the region and far less likely to not graduate at all. Socioeconomic disparities are evident in both attainment and mobility.
Special Education Status (Cramér’s V = 0.255)
Students who received special education services are overwhelmingly more likely to not graduate and far less likely to complete postsecondary education in any location. Disparities extend beyond completion into reduced geographic mobility.
Moderate Predictors
Race/Ethnicity (Cramér’s V = 0.173)
Race and ethnicity meaningfully shape graduation location. White students are more likely to graduate within the region or elsewhere in Minnesota, while American Indian, Black, and Hispanic students are more likely to not graduate. Asian/PI students show comparatively higher rates of graduation outside Minnesota. Disparities influence both attainment and geographic movement.
Gender (Cramér’s V = 0.125)
Women are more likely than men to graduate within the region and elsewhere in Minnesota and are less likely to not graduate. Differences in out-of-state graduation are smaller. Gender gaps are present but less pronounced than socioeconomic or special education disparities.
CTE Course Volume (ANOVA, p < .001)
CTE intensity shows a moderate relationship with graduation location. Students graduating outside the region tend to take fewer CTE courses, while non-completers take more. CTE pathways appear somewhat aligned with regional retention patterns.
Weak Contextual Predictors
Economic Development Region (Cramér’s V = 0.076)
Regional differences in graduation location are statistically detectable but modest. EDR 2 – Headwaters shows higher non-completion and lower regional graduation, but overall explanatory power is limited.
Rural-ness of District (Cramér’s V = 0.053)
District rural classification has a small relationship with graduation location.
County Unemployment and Wage Context (ANOVA, p < .001)
Local economic conditions during high school are statistically associated with graduation location, but differences are extremely small and offer little practical explanation for mobility patterns.
Overall Pattern
Postsecondary graduation location in Northwest Minnesota is shaped primarily by academic readiness, early college exposure, and structural demographic inequities. Students with stronger academic preparation are more likely to complete postsecondary education and more likely to graduate outside the region. Socioeconomic and special education disparities significantly constrain both attainment and mobility. In contrast, high school contextual characteristics contribute little to explaining where students ultimately graduate.

title: Post-secondary pathways
section: 1. Cross-Tier Synthesis (Completion, Credential Level, Sector, and Location)
text:
1. Cross-Tier Synthesis (Completion, Credential Level, Sector, and Location)
Across Tier 1 and Tier 2 outcomes, a clear structure emerges in how postsecondary pathways unfold in Northwest Minnesota. Whether examining completion (ps.grad), credential level (highest.cred.level), institutional sector (ps.grad.InstitutionSector), or graduation location (ps.grad.location), the same drivers consistently shape outcomes.
Students with stronger academic preparation and early exposure to college are far more likely to complete postsecondary education, earn bachelor’s degrees, graduate from 4-year public institutions, and do so outside the region. Conversely, students facing socioeconomic disadvantage, special education barriers, or weaker academic readiness are disproportionately concentrated among non-completers and 2-year institutional pathways, with more limited geographic mobility.
Importantly, stratification intensifies as outcomes become more selective. Gaps widen when moving from completion to bachelor’s attainment and from 2-year to 4-year institutional pathways. Tier 2 outcomes reinforce and amplify patterns observed in Tier 1.

title: Post-secondary pathways
text:
2. Ranked Predictors Across All Outcomes
Strongest and Most Consistent Predictors Across All Outcomes - PSEO participation
- ACT Composite score and MCA proficiency
These variables consistently and powerfully differentiate completion, credential level, institutional tier, and geographic mobility.
Strong and Structural Predictors - Economic status
- Special education status
- Race/Ethnicity
These predictors reflect persistent structural inequities that influence not only whether students complete postsecondary education, but also how far they go and where they graduate.
Moderate / Pathway-Aligned Predictors - CTE achievement
- Total CTE courses taken
CTE participation aligns more strongly with 2-year public pathways and regional retention patterns than with bachelor’s attainment or out-of-region mobility.
Weak Contextual Predictors - Economic Development Region (EDR)
- Rural-ness of school district
- County unemployment
- County wage levels
High school contextual and local economic characteristics are statistically detectable but explain comparatively little variation in postsecondary outcomes.

title: Post-secondary pathways
text:
3. Core Findings
Three overarching conclusions emerge from the full analysis:

Academic readiness is the dominant sorting mechanism.
Students with stronger academic preparation consistently access higher-tier institutions, earn higher credentials, and demonstrate greater geographic mobility.
Early college exposure is the most powerful policy lever.
PSEO participation dramatically shifts outcomes upward across every postsecondary measure examined.
Structural inequities shape both attainment and mobility.
Socioeconomic disadvantage, special education status, and racial disparities meaningfully constrain both completion and access to higher-tier and more mobile postsecondary pathways.

Postsecondary outcomes in the region are not random—they are patterned, predictable, and strongly structured by readiness and access.

title: Post-secondary pathways
section: 4. Implications for Policy and Regional Strategy
text:
4. Implications for Policy and Regional Strategy
The findings suggest several strategic considerations:

Expanding access to rigorous academic preparation and strengthening readiness pipelines may have the largest long-term impact on both completion and bachelor’s production.
Increasing equitable access to PSEO could serve as a high-leverage intervention for shifting students into higher-tier and more mobile pathways.
Addressing socioeconomic and special education disparities must remain central to any strategy aimed at increasing regional attainment.
CTE pathways play an important role in supporting 2-year and locally aligned workforce outcomes, but they are not substitutes for academic preparation when the goal is bachelor’s attainment.
Geographic context alone does not explain postsecondary outcomes; student-level preparation and structural inequities matter far more than rural classification or local labor market conditions.

Taken together, this analysis indicates that improving academic readiness and expanding equitable access to early college opportunities are the most direct levers for strengthening postsecondary success and long-term workforce outcomes in Northwest Minnesota.

title: Post-secondary pathways
section: Most Important Variables Related to Graduating Within the Region
text:
Most Important Variables Related to Graduating Within the Region
The variables most strongly and consistently associated with graduating within the region were:
PSEO Participation
Early college exposure dramatically increased the likelihood of postsecondary completion, which in turn increased the likelihood of graduating within the region. PSEO is one of the most powerful levers influencing regional graduation outcomes.
Academic Readiness (ACT and MCA Scores)
Students with stronger academic preparation were far more likely to complete postsecondary education, including within the region. While the very highest scorers were more geographically mobile, academic readiness clearly separated regional graduates from non-completers.
Economic Status
Socioeconomic disadvantage substantially reduced the likelihood of regional graduation, largely through its strong relationship with non-completion.
Special Education Status
Students who received special education services were far less likely to graduate in any location, including within the region. This was one of the strongest structural constraints observed.

title: Post-secondary pathways
section: Most Important Variables Related to 2-Year / Associate-Level Pathways
text:
Most Important Variables Related to 2-Year / Associate-Level Pathways
The variables most strongly associated with graduating from a 2-year institution and/or earning an associate degree or lesser credential were:
Academic Readiness (ACT and MCA Scores)
Academic preparation strongly sorted students into institutional tiers. Students in 2-year pathways had significantly lower academic performance than those in 4-year public institutions, though higher than non-completers.
PSEO Participation
Students who did not participate in PSEO were much more likely to follow 2-year pathways or not complete. PSEO participation strongly shifted students toward bachelor’s attainment.
CTE Participation and Course Volume
Higher CTE course-taking aligned closely with 2-year public institutional pathways and associate-level attainment. CTE intensity was one of the clearest pathway-alignment variables.
Economic Status and Special Education Status
Structural inequities were also evident in 2-year pathways. Economically disadvantaged students and students receiving special education services were more concentrated in associate-level or non-completion outcomes than in bachelor’s pathways.

Next we need to create a dataset that only has students who graduated from a high school located in EDR 1, 2 and 4. To do that, we will need to join up with the K12Organization dataset to get each person’s school location.

  group_by(NCESID) %>%
  fill(SiteCountyCode, .direction = "downup") %>%

This dataset provides each school institution as of June 29, 2024. This isn’t a list of each school but rather a list of each K12OrganizationID. Each school will have multiple K12OrganizationID.
My goal is to join my standardized region and county code data with this document which will then be joined to the master enrollment document. Unfortunately the main data does not include county name but rather their own code for county. So I will join their codes with the county names and then use my “counties.regions” data to bring together the remaining regions.


k12.org.master <- k12.org.original %>%
  select(K12OrganizationID, SiteCountyCode, OrganizationName) %>%
  left_join(k12.county.codes[,c(1,2)], by = c("SiteCountyCode" = "county.code")) %>%

  mutate(county.name = str_replace(county.name, " County", "")) %>%
  select(K12OrganizationID, OrganizationName, county.name) %>%
  mutate(county.name = str_replace(county.name, "Saint Louis", "St. Louis")) %>%
  left_join(counties.regions.1, by = c("county.name" = "Name")) %>%
  select(-Dem_RUCA, -edr.simple, -mif)
