The dataset now has 262,469 instances that provide a number of characteristics about an individuals enrollment.
My goal is to end up with a dataset where each observation (row) represents a unique (unduplicated) individual that’s tied to the school from which they graduated. Here’s how I will do that.

PersonID: change to integer
K12OrganizationID: change to integer.
K12EnrTimeID: Change so it’s only the year
Grade: eliminate - unimportant since we are mostly concerned about whether they graduated.
EconomicStatusIndicator: I will change this to two categories. Whether they were ever eligible for reduce priced or free meal will be coded as 1 (eligible = codes 1, 2, 4, 5) at any point in the dataset. The remaining will be coded as 0.
FederalRaceIndicator: eliminate - I’m going to use the RaceEthnicity column instead.
PSEO columns: I’m most concerned about whether they participated in PSEO or not. A new column will be created indicated whether there was a “Y” coded for either the “PSEOConcurrentEnrollmentIndicator” or “PostSecondaryEnrollmentOptionsIndicator” columns at any point in the dataset. The two original columns will be eliminated.
StatusEnd: I will only include individuals that have StatusEnd coded as an 8 or 9.
Gender: included and not changed.
SpecialEdStatus: I will split this into two categories. The first is that a student did not require special education services (codes 1-2) at any point in the dataset. The other category will be that they require special education (codes 4 - 9) at any point in the dataset.
Limited English: keep with no changes.
Primary language: I’m going to create two categories. One being that English is the primary language while the other will be that English is NOT the primary language at any point in the dataset.
Homeless: Keep with no changes.

We first need to create a list of all individuals with the StatusEnd code of 8 or 9 (graduated). In addition, we need to remove any student that graduated before 2008. This is because we will not have their full Sophmore - Senior records which is especially important when attempting to identify whether the number of CTE courses taken predicts migration.
The other element I want to add is a pre-covid and post-covid variable. All individuals whose grad year is before 2020 will be labeled pre-covid and all others wil lbe post-covid.
This “graduates list” will be the list on which we will bind all the other variables.


graduates <- enrollment.org.northwest %>%
  filter(StatusEnd %in% c(8,9)) %>%
  mutate(grad.year = str_sub(TimeID, 1, 4),
         grad.year = as.integer(grad.year),
         grad.year.covid = ifelse(grad.year > 2019, "Post-covid grad", "Pre-covid grad")) %>%
  select(PersonID, K12OrganizationID, OrganizationName, grad.year, grad.year.covid, Gender, LimitedEnglishProficiencyIndicator, HomelessIndicator, county.name, countyfp, Dem_Desc, edr) %>%
  distinct(PersonID, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%
  filter(grad.year > 2007) 


Next we need to create new columns for the variables that will indicate a certain status has been met at any point in the dataset for each PersonID. For example, we want a column that indicates a PersonID was enrolled in PSEO at some point in the dataset. The variables that need a new column created are;

EconomicStatusIndicator
PostSecondaryEnrollmentOptionsIndicator & PSEOConcurrentEnrollmentIndicator
SpecialEdStatus
HomeprimaryLanguage



# Lets start with economic status indicator
k12.enr.esi.indicator <- enrollment.org.northwest %>%
  select(PersonID, EconomicStatusIndicator) %>%
  group_by(PersonID) %>%
  mutate(economic.status.code = ifelse(EconomicStatusIndicator %in% c(1,2,4,5), 1, 0),
         economic.status.code.sum = sum(economic.status.code)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  distinct(PersonID, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%
  mutate(economic.status = ifelse(economic.status.code.sum > 0, 1, 0)) %>%
  select(PersonID, economic.status)

# PSEO

k12.enr.pseo.indicator <- enrollment.org.northwest %>%
  select(PersonID, PostSecondaryEnrollmentOptionsIndicator, PSEOConcurrentEnrollmentIndicator) %>%
  mutate(PostSecondaryEnrollmentOptionsIndicator = ifelse(PostSecondaryEnrollmentOptionsIndicator == "Y", 1, 0),
         PSEOConcurrentEnrollmentIndicator = ifelse(PSEOConcurrentEnrollmentIndicator == "Y", 1, 0)) %>%
  gather(key = "pseo.type", value = "indicator", 2:3) %>%
  group_by(PersonID) %>%
  summarise(pseo = sum(indicator)) %>%
  mutate(pseo.participant = ifelse(pseo > 0, 1, 0)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(PersonID, pseo.participant)

# Special Ed status

k12.enr.spced.indicator <- enrollment.org.northwest %>%
  select(PersonID, SpecialEdStatus) %>%
  mutate(SpecialEdStatus.code = ifelse(SpecialEdStatus %in% c(4,5,6,7,8,9), 1, 0)) %>%
  group_by(PersonID) %>%
  summarise(SpecialEdStatus.sum = sum(SpecialEdStatus.code)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(SpecialEdStatus = ifelse(SpecialEdStatus.sum > 0, 1, 0)) %>%
  select(PersonID, SpecialEdStatus)

# Home Primary Language

k12.enr.homeprimlang.indicator <- enrollment.org.northwest %>%
  select(PersonID, HomePrimaryLanguage) %>%
  mutate(HomePrimaryLanguage.code = ifelse(HomePrimaryLanguage == 11, 0, 1)) %>%
  group_by(PersonID) %>%
  summarise(HomePrimaryLanguage.sum = sum(HomePrimaryLanguage.code)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(non.english.home = ifelse(HomePrimaryLanguage.sum > 0, 1, 0)) %>%
  select(PersonID, non.english.home)

# Race

k12.enr.race <- enrollment.org.northwest %>%
  select(PersonID, RaceEthnicity) %>%
  mutate(RaceEthnicity = ifelse(RaceEthnicity == 0, "Unknown",
                                ifelse(RaceEthnicity == 1, "AI",
                                       ifelse(RaceEthnicity == 2, "Asian/PI",
                                              ifelse(RaceEthnicity == 3, "Hispanic",
                                                     ifelse(RaceEthnicity == 4, "Black", "White"))))),
         RaceEthnicity = as.factor(RaceEthnicity)) %>%
  distinct(PersonID, RaceEthnicity) %>%
  mutate(code = ifelse(RaceEthnicity == "Unknown", 0, 1)) %>%
  group_by(PersonID) %>%
  filter(code == max(code)) %>%
  ungroup %>%
  distinct(PersonID, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%
  select(PersonID, RaceEthnicity)


Now we can combine the list of graduates with the status indicators.


k12.enrollment.master <- graduates %>%
  left_join(k12.enr.esi.indicator, by = "PersonID") %>%
  left_join(k12.enr.pseo.indicator, by = "PersonID") %>%
  left_join(k12.enr.spced.indicator, by = "PersonID") %>%
  left_join(k12.enr.homeprimlang.indicator, by = "PersonID") %>%
  left_join(k12.enr.race, by = "PersonID") %>%
  select(PersonID, K12OrganizationID, OrganizationName, county.name, countyfp, Dem_Desc, edr, grad.year, grad.year.covid, Gender, LimitedEnglishProficiencyIndicator, HomelessIndicator, economic.status, pseo.participant, SpecialEdStatus, non.english.home, RaceEthnicity)

kable(head(k12.enrollment.master))

Summary of K12Enrollment data
Below is the percentage of total observations that;

Were eligible for free or reduced lunch at some point in the dataset
Are Female
Have been homeless at some point in the dataset
Had limited english proficiency at some point in the dataset

As expected, nearly half (49.2%) of the observations are female. Over a third (35.5%) of the observations have been eligible for free or reduced lunch at some point in the dataset. Also, over a quarter (26.4%) of observations participated in a PSEO course. Nearly 30% are non-white students , 15% are categorized as needing special education, 3% have non-english speakers at home, 1% have limited english proficiency and nearly 1% were categorized as homeless at some point between Sophmore and Senior year.

title: Pathways After High School – Northwest Minnesota
text:
This research examined which factors most strongly influence whether individuals achieve meaningful employment within Northwestern Minnesota’s region (EDR 1, 2 & 4) after high school. Using both contingency table analysis (effect sizes, trends over 1, 5, and 10 years) and CART modeling, the goal was to identify which variables most reliably anchor individuals in the regional workforce versus those that push them toward statewide mobility, disconnection, or outmigration.
A special focus was also placed on non-college graduates, who represent a substantial share of the region’s young adults and are at higher risk of disconnection from the labor force. Understanding their pathways provides additional insights into how to build a stronger, more inclusive regional workforce.
The findings provide a roadmap for education and workforce policy, showing how demographics, high school context, accomplishments, and postsecondary experiences interact to shape local labor market attachment.

title: Pathways After High School – Northwest Minnesota
text:
This research examined which factors most strongly influence whether individuals achieve meaningful employment within Northwestern Minnesota’s region (EDR 1, 2 & 4) after high school. Using both contingency table analysis (effect sizes, trends over 1, 5, and 10 years) and CART modeling, the goal was to identify which variables most reliably anchor individuals in the regional workforce versus those that push them toward statewide mobility, disconnection, or outmigration.
A special focus was also placed on non-college graduates, who represent a substantial share of the region’s young adults and are at higher risk of disconnection from the labor force. Understanding their pathways provides additional insights into how to build a stronger, more inclusive regional workforce.
The findings provide a roadmap for education and workforce policy, showing how demographics, high school context, accomplishments, and postsecondary experiences interact to shape local labor market attachment.

title: Pathways After High School – Northwest Minnesota
text:
Relationships - Employment
Across demographic characteristics, high school context, high school accomplishments, and postsecondary experiences, clear and consistent themes emerge about how graduates from Northwest Minnesota connect to the workforce over time. Rather than reflecting a single pipeline, the analysis reveals distinct pathways that balance regional attachment, employment quality, and geographic mobility, with meaningful regional employment serving as a central anchor outcome throughout.
Several overarching patterns stand out:

Postsecondary experiences show the strongest and most persistent associations with workforce outcomes. Whether and where students complete postsecondary education, the type of institution they attend, and the credential they earn all shape long-term regional retention and statewide mobility.
Career and Technical Education (CTE) is the most consistent contributor to meaningful regional employment, supporting early entry into the workforce and sustained regional attachment.
Academic achievement and rigor indicators (ACT, MCA scores, AP participation) are strongly associated with postsecondary enrollment and mobility beyond the region, rather than long-term regional employment.
Demographic and high school contextual factors matter, but primarily by shaping exposure to opportunity and risk rather than directly determining outcomes.
Across nearly all domains, a recurring tension emerges between regional retention and employment quality, underscoring the importance of examining both dimensions together.



1. Strong Predictors of Regional Employment and Disconnection
These variables show small-to-moderate but highly consistent associations with workforce outcomes and clear differentiation in meaningful regional employment across multiple time points.









Variable
Type
Key Insights




ps.grad.location
Postsecondary
Strongest and most persistent association. Graduates completing PS within the region or Northwest EDR are most likely to remain in meaningful regional employment; out-of-state grads are most mobile and disconnected.


ps.grad
Postsecondary
Completion reduces long-term disconnection and increases access to meaningful employment, particularly outside the region.


highest.cred.level
Postsecondary
Sub-baccalaureate credentials align most closely with regional employment; bachelor’s+ credentials are associated with statewide mobility.


ps.grad.InstitutionSector
Postsecondary
Public 2-year colleges show the strongest and most durable connection to meaningful regional employment.


CTE achievement
HS Accomplishment
Concentrators and completers consistently show higher rates of meaningful regional employment.


total.cte.courses.taken
HS Accomplishment
Greater CTE intensity corresponds to stronger and more sustained regional workforce attachment.


Economic status
Demographic
Lower-income students enter the workforce earlier and remain regionally attached but face higher exposure to not-meaningful employment and disconnection.


Special education status
Demographic
Persistent disparities across all timeframes, with higher rates of disconnection and lower access to meaningful employment.





2. Moderate Predictors — Clear Associations with Attenuation or Context Dependence
These variables show consistent but weaker associations, often shaping early pathways or mobility rather than long-term regional attachment.









Variable
Type
Key Insights




attended.ps
Postsecondary
Very large early association; differences narrow substantially by 10 years.


Race/Ethnicity
Demographic
Persistent disparities in mobility and disconnection; patterns vary by group.


Gender
Demographic
Early differences in workforce entry and PS attendance narrow over time.


District rurality (Dem_Desc)
HS Characteristic
Rural graduates more likely to remain regionally employed; urban grads more mobile.


Economic Development Region (EDR)
HS Characteristic
Northwest-based districts show stronger regional attachment.


MCA Reading, Math, Science
HS Accomplishment
Academic differentiation aligns with employment quality and mobility rather than regional retention.


ACT composite score
HS Accomplishment
Higher scores strongly associated with PS continuation and statewide employment.


Local unemployment context
HS Characteristic
Higher unemployment areas associated with earlier workforce entry and weaker early outcomes.





3. Weak or Inconsistent Predictors — Limited Independent Signal
These variables show very small or inconsistent associations and offer limited added value outside interaction effects.









Variable
Type
Notes




english.learner
Demographic
Early differences diminish substantially over time.


Limited English Proficiency indicators
Demographic
Overlap with other variables; weak independent association.


non.english.home
Demographic
Small and inconsistent differences.


sat.taken
HS Accomplishment
Small subgroup; weak differentiation.


took.ACT
HS Accomplishment
Near-universal participation limits usefulness.


ap.exam
HS Accomplishment
Moderate overlap with ACT and MCA indicators; limited unique signal.





Overall Conclusion
Taken together, the analysis highlights a dual structure shaping workforce outcomes in Northwest Minnesota:

CTE pathways, public 2-year institutions, sub-baccalaureate credentials, and regionally located postsecondary completion consistently anchor graduates to meaningful employment within the region.
Higher academic achievement and 4-year-oriented pathways are associated with greater geographic mobility and employment outside the region, often at the expense of regional retention.

Demographic and contextual factors shape exposure to opportunity and risk but operate largely through these educational and training pathways rather than independently determining outcomes. Importantly, regional retention does not always coincide with employment quality, emphasizing the need for strategies that both retain graduates locally and expand access to stable, high-quality regional jobs.
Overall, the findings suggest that strengthening the Northwest Minnesota workforce will require intentional alignment between CTE, 2-year postsecondary pathways, and regional employers, alongside targeted supports for populations facing persistent disadvantage.

title: Pathways After High School – Northwest Minnesota
text:
Relationships - Post-secondary

1. Cross-Tier Synthesis (Completion, Credential Level, Sector, and Location)
Across Tier 1 and Tier 2 outcomes, a clear structure emerges in how postsecondary pathways unfold in Northwest Minnesota. Whether examining completion (ps.grad), credential level (highest.cred.level), institutional sector (ps.grad.InstitutionSector), or graduation location (ps.grad.location), the same drivers consistently shape outcomes.
Students with stronger academic preparation and early exposure to college are far more likely to complete postsecondary education, earn bachelor’s degrees, graduate from 4-year public institutions, and do so outside the region. Conversely, students facing socioeconomic disadvantage, special education barriers, or weaker academic readiness are disproportionately concentrated among non-completers and 2-year institutional pathways, with more limited geographic mobility.
Importantly, stratification intensifies as outcomes become more selective. Gaps widen when moving from completion to bachelor’s attainment and from 2-year to 4-year institutional pathways. Tier 2 outcomes reinforce and amplify patterns observed in Tier 1.


2. Ranked Predictors Across All Outcomes
Strongest and Most Consistent Predictors Across All Outcomes

PSEO participation
ACT Composite score and MCA proficiency

These variables consistently and powerfully differentiate completion, credential level, institutional tier, and geographic mobility.
Strong and Structural Predictors

Economic status
Special education status
Race/Ethnicity

These predictors reflect persistent structural inequities that influence not only whether students complete postsecondary education, but also how far they go and where they graduate.
Moderate / Pathway-Aligned Predictors

CTE achievement
Total CTE courses taken

CTE participation aligns more strongly with 2-year public pathways and regional retention patterns than with bachelor’s attainment or out-of-region mobility.
Weak Contextual Predictors

Economic Development Region (EDR)
Rural-ness of school district
County unemployment
County wage levels

High school contextual and local economic characteristics are statistically detectable but explain comparatively little variation in postsecondary outcomes.


3. Core Findings
Three overarching conclusions emerge from the full analysis:

Academic readiness is the dominant sorting mechanism.
Students with stronger academic preparation consistently access higher-tier institutions, earn higher credentials, and demonstrate greater geographic mobility.
Early college exposure is the most powerful policy lever.
PSEO participation dramatically shifts outcomes upward across every postsecondary measure examined.
Structural inequities shape both attainment and mobility.
Socioeconomic disadvantage, special education status, and racial disparities meaningfully constrain both completion and access to higher-tier and more mobile postsecondary pathways.

Postsecondary outcomes in the region are not random—they are patterned, predictable, and strongly structured by readiness and access.

4. Implications for Policy and Regional Strategy
The findings suggest several strategic considerations:

Expanding access to rigorous academic preparation and strengthening readiness pipelines may have the largest long-term impact on both completion and bachelor’s production.
Increasing equitable access to PSEO could serve as a high-leverage intervention for shifting students into higher-tier and more mobile pathways.
Addressing socioeconomic and special education disparities must remain central to any strategy aimed at increasing regional attainment.
CTE pathways play an important role in supporting 2-year and locally aligned workforce outcomes, but they are not substitutes for academic preparation when the goal is bachelor’s attainment.
Geographic context alone does not explain postsecondary outcomes; student-level preparation and structural inequities matter far more than rural classification or local labor market conditions.

Taken together, this analysis indicates that improving academic readiness and expanding equitable access to early college opportunities are the most direct levers for strengthening postsecondary success and long-term workforce outcomes in Northwest Minnesota.



Core questions

Most Important Variables Related to Graduating Within the Region
The variables most strongly and consistently associated with graduating within the region were:
PSEO Participation
Early college exposure dramatically increased the likelihood of postsecondary completion, which in turn increased the likelihood of graduating within the region. PSEO is one of the most powerful levers influencing regional graduation outcomes.
Academic Readiness (ACT and MCA Scores)
Students with stronger academic preparation were far more likely to complete postsecondary education, including within the region. While the very highest scorers were more geographically mobile, academic readiness clearly separated regional graduates from non-completers.
Economic Status
Socioeconomic disadvantage substantially reduced the likelihood of regional graduation, largely through its strong relationship with non-completion.
Special Education Status
Students who received special education services were far less likely to graduate in any location, including within the region. This was one of the strongest structural constraints observed.


Most Important Variables Related to 2-Year / Associate-Level Pathways
The variables most strongly associated with graduating from a 2-year institution and/or earning an associate degree or lesser credential were:
Academic Readiness (ACT and MCA Scores)
Academic preparation strongly sorted students into institutional tiers. Students in 2-year pathways had significantly lower academic performance than those in 4-year public institutions, though higher than non-completers.
PSEO Participation
Students who did not participate in PSEO were much more likely to follow 2-year pathways or not complete. PSEO participation strongly shifted students toward bachelor’s attainment.
CTE Participation and Course Volume
Higher CTE course-taking aligned closely with 2-year public institutional pathways and associate-level attainment. CTE intensity was one of the clearest pathway-alignment variables.
Economic Status and Special Education Status
Structural inequities were also evident in 2-year pathways. Economically disadvantaged students and students receiving special education services were more concentrated in associate-level or non-completion outcomes than in bachelor’s pathways.

title: Pathways After High School – Northwest Minnesota
text:
Multivariate - Employment CART
Across five logistic regression models—four predicting sustained regional employment and one predicting regional postsecondary graduation—a coherent and layered mechanism emerges. Regional workforce retention is not explained by academic preparation alone. Instead, it reflects the interaction of postsecondary geography, institutional sector, and career-aligned high school pathways, operating within persistent structural inequalities.


Big Takeaways
1. Regional embeddedness is the dominant retention mechanism.
Graduating from a postsecondary institution within the region—particularly a 2-year public college—is the strongest predictor of sustained regional employment. Students completing a 2-year public credential within the region exhibit roughly 110% higher odds of sustained regional employment compared to non-graduates. Regional graduation across sectors increases retention, but the effect is strongest for community and technical colleges.
2. CTE is the strongest high school-level workforce predictor and operates independently.
CTE concentrators and completers exhibit approximately 60% higher odds of sustained regional employment, even after controlling for credential level, institutional sector, and graduation location. While CTE modestly increases the likelihood of regional graduation (~20% higher odds), its workforce retention effect is substantially larger—indicating that CTE functions as a direct workforce-alignment pathway rather than solely a feeder into regional postsecondary completion.
3. Four-year pathways—especially outside the region—align with outward mobility.
Graduating from 4-year institutions outside the region is associated with substantially lower odds of sustained regional employment. Bachelor’s degree attainment, once geography is considered, is consistently associated with outward labor market mobility.
4. Academic readiness operates through postsecondary pathways.
Math proficiency and PSEO participation show modest baseline associations with workforce outcomes but attenuate once postsecondary factors are included. Their effects primarily operate through postsecondary attainment and geography rather than directly shaping long-term regional attachment.
5. Structural disparities emerge early and persist.
Racial and ethnic disparities are evident in both regional graduation and regional employment models. Black and Asian/PI students exhibit roughly 45–50% lower odds of sustained regional employment relative to White students, and similar disparities appear in regional postsecondary graduation. These gaps are not explained by credential level, institutional sector, or geography alone, indicating deeper structural inequalities in regional pathways.



Relative Strength of Predictors (Ranked by Magnitude of Association)
The table below ranks predictors by approximate strength of association (distance from OR = 1) in the final sector × location model and supplemental graduation model.









Rank
Predictor
Direction
Approximate Effect on Sustained Regional Employment




1
2-year public × Regional graduation
Strong positive
~110% higher odds


2
4-year public × Regional graduation
Positive
~58% higher odds


3
CTE Concentrator/Completor
Positive
~60% higher odds


4
2-year public × Outside region
Positive
~47% higher odds


5
4-year public × Outside region
Negative
~31% lower odds


6
Other sector × Outside region
Strong negative
~59% lower odds


7
Race (Black, Asian/PI)
Negative
~45–50% lower odds


8
CTE Participant
Positive
~28% higher odds


9
Hispanic ethnicity
Negative
~32% lower odds


10
Economic disadvantage (regional graduation model)
Negative
~32% lower odds


11
Special Education status (regional graduation model)
Negative
~53% lower odds (for regional graduation)


12
PSEO participation
Small positive (regional grad) / small negative (employment)
~40% ↑ regional grad; minimal independent workforce effect


13
MCA Math
Small positive (regional grad)
~6% higher odds per unit; attenuated in workforce model






Overall Conclusion
Regional workforce retention is driven most strongly by locally embedded postsecondary institutions—especially 2-year public colleges—combined with career-aligned high school pathways such as CTE. Academic readiness influences retention primarily through postsecondary trajectories, while four-year pathways, particularly outside the region, align with outward mobility. Persistent racial and structural disparities emerge at the postsecondary graduation stage and remain evident in workforce outcomes.
In short, sustained regional employment reflects a locally anchored educational-to-workforce pipeline shaped by institutional embeddedness and career alignment, operating within broader structural inequality.

  left_join(dep.var.names, by = "PersonID") %>%
  mutate_at(vars(Dem_Desc:ps.grad.in.MN), as.factor) %>%
  mutate_at(vars(grad.year.1:grad.year.10), as.factor) %>%
  mutate(grad.year.1 = fct_relevel(grad.year.1, "Meaningful emp County", "Meaningful emp EDR", "Meaningful emp region", "Meaningful emp MN", "Attending ps", "Not meaningful, not attending ps", "No MN emp record, not attending ps"),
         grad.year.5 = fct_relevel(grad.year.5, "Meaningful emp County", "Meaningful emp EDR", "Meaningful emp region", "Meaningful emp MN", "Attending ps", "Not meaningful, not attending ps", "No MN emp record, not attending ps"),
         grad.year.10 = fct_relevel(grad.year.10, "Meaningful emp County", "Meaningful emp EDR", "Meaningful emp region", "Meaningful emp MN", "Attending ps", "Not meaningful, not attending ps", "No MN emp record, not attending ps"),
         highest.cred.level = ifelse(ps.grad.InstitutionSector == "Did not grad", "Some college", as.character(highest.cred.level)),
         highest.cred.level = ifelse(ps.grad.InstitutionSector == "Never attended ps", "No college", as.character(highest.cred.level)),
         highest.cred.level = as.factor(highest.cred.level),
         Dem_Desc = fct_relevel(Dem_Desc, "Town/rural mix", "Urban/town/rural mix", "Entirely urban")) %>%
  droplevels()

There are a number of “states” in which an individual in this dataset are assigned;
There are three “times” in which we checked to see if the individual had meaningful employment within these geographies;
Meaningful employment is determined by whether an individual worked 1,000 hours for an employer during time X. In addition, if the individual is working for an employer but not meaningful at time X but worked 1,000 hours for that employer during another year it’s still considered “meaningful”.
Due to time x potentially being passed the date of the latest data (2023) for some individuals, the analysis below will filter out all individuals where time x is after 2023.
From the multiple correspondence analysis and the principle component analysis we determined that the following independent variables will be included in the analysis.
updated.master %>%
  select(-PersonID, -grad.year.1, -grad.year.5, -grad.year.10) %>%
  lapply(class)

title: Local Employment Cross-tabs
text:
Summary of all demographic variables
Across the six demographic characteristics examined—economic status, special education status, English learner status, home language, gender, and race/ethnicity—there are statistically significant relationships with post-graduation workforce outcomes in Northwest Minnesota. While all variables are associated with differences in workforce pathways, the strength and consistency of these relationships vary, with some demographic characteristics showing substantially larger differentiation across outcomes than others.
Relative Strength of Relationships
Among the demographic characteristics examined, economic status and special education status show the largest and most consistent associations with workforce outcomes, particularly in the early years following high school graduation. These variables display the greatest separation across outcome categories, including meaningful regional employment, not-meaningful employment, postsecondary participation, and disconnection from Minnesota employment records.
English learner status and home language show moderate associations with workforce outcomes. Differences across these groups are smaller than those observed for economic status and special education status but remain consistent across timepoints. In particular, these variables are associated with meaningful differences in long-term regional workforce attachment, with widening separation in rates of meaningful regional employment and no Minnesota employment record over time.
In contrast, gender and race/ethnicity exhibit modest associations with workforce outcomes at the aggregate level. While statistically significant, these relationships show smaller overall differentiation across outcome categories. That said, both variables are associated with persistent differences in regional workforce attachment and employment quality, even as gaps narrow over time.
Cross-Cutting Patterns in Workforce Outcomes
Several patterns recur across demographic characteristics. Groups with higher rates of meaningful regional employment one year after graduation—including lower-income graduates, special education graduates, males, and some racial and ethnic groups—also tend to show higher concentrations of not-meaningful employment and lower rates of postsecondary participation. Early regional workforce attachment, therefore, often coincides with greater exposure to lower-quality or less stable employment.
Conversely, demographic groups with stronger connections to postsecondary education—including higher-income graduates, females, and White graduates—are less likely to enter meaningful regional employment immediately after high school but are also less exposed to early not-meaningful work. Over time, many individuals in these groups transition into meaningful employment, often outside the Northwest region, contributing to lower long-term regional retention.
Language-related demographic characteristics—English learner status and home language—stand out for their association with long-term detachment from the regional labor market. Although early regional employment rates may be comparable at one year after graduation, these groups show substantially lower rates of meaningful regional employment and higher rates of no Minnesota employment record by ten years after graduation.
Bringing the Demographic Findings Together
Taken together, these results indicate that demographic characteristics are differentially associated with workforce participation outcomes in Northwest Minnesota. Economic status and special education status are most closely linked to early and persistent differences in workforce pathways, while language-related characteristics are associated with growing disparities in regional retention over time. Gender and race/ethnicity play a more modest but still meaningful role, particularly with respect to employment quality and regional attachment.
Overall, the demographic findings highlight a central tension in the regional workforce: groups that are more likely to enter the regional workforce early are also more likely to experience not-meaningful employment, while groups that delay workforce entry through postsecondary education are more likely to secure higher-quality employment later—often outside the region. Addressing this imbalance will require strategies that strengthen durable, high-quality regional employment pathways, particularly for demographic groups most exposed to early instability and long-term detachment.

title: Local Employment Cross-tabs
text:
Gender

Key Findings
There is a statistically significant relationship between gender and post-graduation workforce outcomes in all three timeframes:

1 year after graduation: χ²(4) = 736.09, Cramer’s V = 0.109, p < .001, N = 61,485
5 years after graduation: χ²(4) = 371.19, Cramer’s V = 0.090, p < .001, N = 45,359
10 years after graduation: χ²(4) = 84.53, Cramer’s V = 0.058, p < .001, N = 25,464

The strength of the relationship is moderate one year after graduation and weakens over time, though it remains statistically significant across all three intervals.
Interpretation Over Time
1 Year After Graduation:
Gender is associated with clear differences in early post-graduation pathways, including access to meaningful regional employment. Males are more likely than females to be engaged in meaningful employment within the region, indicating stronger early regional workforce attachment. Females, in contrast, are substantially more likely to be attending postsecondary education, reflecting delayed workforce entry. Males are also more likely to have no Minnesota employment record, while females are less represented in disconnection and not-meaningful employment at this stage. Together, these patterns suggest gendered differences in the timing and mode of entry into the regional workforce.
5 Years After Graduation:
Five years after graduation, gender-based differences persist but are less pronounced. Males continue to show higher rates of meaningful regional employment, indicating sustained regional attachment. Females remain more likely to be attending postsecondary education, though at much lower levels than at one year. Females are also slightly more represented in not-meaningful employment, while males continue to show somewhat higher rates of no Minnesota employment record. These patterns reflect different trajectories toward stable employment rather than differences in overall labor force participation.
10 Years After Graduation:
Ten years after graduation, gender differences narrow further but remain evident. Males retain a modest advantage in meaningful regional employment, while rates of meaningful employment elsewhere in Minnesota are nearly identical by gender. Females are more likely to be in not-meaningful employment, suggesting persistent differences in employment quality rather than employment access. Rates of having no Minnesota employment record remain high for both groups, with minimal gender differences. At this stage, the primary gender distinction relates to regional workforce anchoring and job quality.
Conclusion
Gender is consistently associated with differences in workforce pathways in Northwest Minnesota. Males show higher rates of early and sustained meaningful regional employment, while females are more likely to delay workforce entry through postsecondary education and are somewhat more concentrated in not-meaningful employment later on. Although these differences diminish over time, a small but persistent gender gap in regional meaningful employment remains.


One year after graduatingFive years after graduatingTen years after graduating

title: Local Employment Cross-tabs
text:
economic.status

Key Findings
There is a statistically significant relationship between economic status and post-graduation workforce outcomes in all three timeframes:

1 year after graduation: χ²(4) = 3967.83, Cramer’s V = 0.254, p < .001, N = 61,485
5 years after graduation: χ²(4) = 744.99, Cramer’s V = 0.128, p < .001, N = 45,359
10 years after graduation: χ²(4) = 169.97, Cramer’s V = 0.082, p < .001, N = 25,464

The relationship is strongest one year after graduation and weakens over time, though it remains statistically significant across all three intervals.
Interpretation Over Time
1 Year After Graduation:
Economic status is strongly associated with early post-graduation pathways, including access to meaningful regional employment. Graduates eligible for free or reduced-price lunch are more likely to be engaged in meaningful employment within the region (16.4% vs. 11.2%), indicating earlier regional workforce attachment. However, this higher regional employment share coincides with substantially higher representation in not-meaningful employment and not attending postsecondary education (27.1% vs. 14.5%) and a higher likelihood of having no Minnesota employment record (17.6% vs. 10.9%). In contrast, graduates not eligible for free or reduced-price lunch are far more likely to be attending postsecondary education (61.0% vs. 34.8%), delaying workforce entry while reducing early exposure to low-quality employment. These patterns indicate meaningful differences in the quality and stability of early regional workforce attachment by economic status.
5 Years After Graduation:
By five years after graduation, differences by economic status narrow but remain substantial. Rates of meaningful regional employment increase for both groups and remain slightly higher among graduates eligible for free or reduced-price lunch (25.2% vs. 22.3%), reflecting continued regional attachment. At the same time, economically disadvantaged graduates remain more likely to be in not-meaningful employment (22.6% vs. 16.1%) and slightly more likely to have no Minnesota employment record. Graduates not eligible for free or reduced-price lunch are more likely to hold meaningful employment elsewhere in Minnesota, suggesting differences in geographic mobility and access to higher-quality opportunities outside the region.
10 Years After Graduation:
Ten years after graduation, rates of meaningful regional employment converge across economic status groups, with approximately one-quarter of graduates in each group working in meaningful jobs within the region. Despite this convergence, important differences persist. Graduates eligible for free or reduced-price lunch remain more likely to be in not-meaningful employment (20.3% vs. 15.0%), while graduates not eligible are more likely to hold meaningful employment elsewhere in Minnesota. Rates of having no Minnesota employment record remain high for both groups. These patterns suggest that economic status continues to be associated with employment quality and mobility, even when regional workforce attachment appears similar.
Conclusion
Economic status is consistently associated with differences in workforce pathways in Northwest Minnesota. Graduates from lower-income backgrounds are more likely to enter the regional workforce earlier and to remain regionally attached, but they are also far more likely to be concentrated in not-meaningful employment and disconnected from postsecondary education, particularly in the years immediately following graduation. Although differences in regional employment rates narrow over time, persistent disparities in employment quality and stability remain evident.

title: Local Employment Cross-tabs
text:
SpecialEdStatus

Key Findings
There is a statistically significant relationship between special education status and post-graduation workforce outcomes in all three timeframes:

1 year after graduation: χ²(4) = 4284.25, Cramer’s V = 0.264, p < .001, N = 61,485
5 years after graduation: χ²(4) = 936.62, Cramer’s V = 0.144, p < .001, N = 45,359
10 years after graduation: χ²(4) = 158.37, Cramer’s V = 0.079, p < .001, N = 25,464

The relationship is strongest one year after graduation and weakens over time, though it remains statistically significant across all three intervals.
Interpretation Over Time
1 Year After Graduation:
Special education status is strongly associated with early post-graduation pathways, including access to meaningful regional employment. Graduates who received special education services are more likely to be engaged in meaningful employment within the region (17.6% vs. 12.3%), indicating earlier regional workforce attachment. At the same time, these graduates are far less likely to be attending postsecondary education (20.9% vs. 56.8%) and are substantially more likely to be in not-meaningful employment and not attending postsecondary education (31.1% vs. 17.0%) or to have no Minnesota employment record (26.5% vs. 11.1%). These patterns highlight large differences in the stability and quality of early workforce attachment associated with special education status.
5 Years After Graduation:
Five years after graduation, rates of meaningful regional employment increase for both groups and remain slightly higher among graduates who received special education services (25.8% vs. 22.9%). Despite this continued regional attachment, substantial differences persist. Special education graduates remain much less likely to be attending postsecondary education (6.2% vs. 19.7%) and are more likely to be in not-meaningful employment (25.8% vs. 17.2%) or to have no Minnesota employment record (34.4% vs. 28.7%). These differences indicate continued disparities in employment quality and stability, even as workforce participation increases.
10 Years After Graduation:
Ten years after graduation, differences narrow but remain evident. Graduates who received special education services are slightly more likely to hold meaningful regional employment (26.2% vs. 23.9%), suggesting sustained regional attachment. However, they remain less likely to have meaningful employment elsewhere in Minnesota (9.2% vs. 15.7%) and more likely to be in not-meaningful employment (21.9% vs. 16.0%). Rates of having no Minnesota employment record remain high for both groups, with modest differences. These patterns indicate long-term differences in employment quality and geographic mobility associated with special education status.
Conclusion
Special education status is consistently associated with differences in workforce pathways in Northwest Minnesota. Graduates who received special education services are more likely to enter the regional workforce earlier and to remain regionally attached over time, but they are also far more likely to experience not-meaningful employment and employment instability. Although differences in regional employment rates narrow over time, persistent gaps in job quality and access to opportunities outside the region remain evident.

title: Local Employment Cross-tabs
text:
RaceEthnicity

Key Findings
There is a statistically significant relationship between race/ethnicity and post-graduation workforce outcomes in all three timeframes:

1 year after graduation: χ²(20) = 1811.69, Cramer’s V = 0.086, p < .001, N = 61,485
5 years after graduation: χ²(16) = 444.03, Cramer’s V = 0.049, p < .001, N = 45,359
10 years after graduation: χ²(16) = 237.64, Cramer’s V = 0.048, p < .001, N = 25,464

The relationship is modest in magnitude and remains statistically significant across all three intervals.
Interpretation Over Time
1 Year After Graduation:
Race and ethnicity are associated with clear differences in early post-graduation pathways, including access to meaningful regional employment. White and American Indian graduates show relatively higher rates of meaningful employment within the region, while Black, Hispanic, and Asian/Pacific Islander graduates are less likely to be regionally employed in meaningful jobs. At the same time, White graduates are substantially more likely to be attending postsecondary education, while non-White graduates—particularly American Indian, Black, and Hispanic graduates—are more likely to be in not-meaningful employment or to have no Minnesota employment record. These patterns indicate meaningful differences in both regional workforce attachment and employment stability across racial and ethnic groups immediately following high school.
5 Years After Graduation:
By five years after graduation, rates of meaningful regional employment increase across all racial and ethnic groups, but disparities persist. White and American Indian graduates continue to show stronger regional attachment through meaningful employment, while Black and Asian/Pacific Islander graduates remain less likely to be employed in meaningful regional roles. Hispanic graduates fall between these groups. Non-White graduates—particularly Black and Hispanic individuals—are more likely to have no Minnesota employment record, suggesting weaker or less stable attachment to the regional labor market. Differences in postsecondary enrollment narrow substantially by this point, shifting disparities primarily toward employment quality and regional retention.
10 Years After Graduation:
Ten years after graduation, racial and ethnic differences narrow further but remain evident. White and American Indian graduates continue to exhibit higher rates of meaningful regional employment, while Black, Hispanic, and Asian/Pacific Islander graduates are less likely to be anchored in meaningful jobs within the region. Across several non-White groups, larger shares of individuals have no Minnesota employment record, indicating continued differences in long-term regional retention. At this stage, racial and ethnic disparities are most visible in regional workforce anchoring and job quality, rather than overall labor force participation.
Conclusion
Race and ethnicity are associated with persistent differences in workforce pathways in Northwest Minnesota. White and American Indian graduates are more likely to secure and maintain meaningful regional employment, while Black, Hispanic, and Asian/Pacific Islander graduates experience lower rates of regional attachment and higher levels of employment instability. Although these differences diminish over time, disparities in regional workforce anchoring and employment quality remain evident a decade after graduation.

title: Local Employment Cross-tabs
text:
non.english.home

Key Findings
There is a statistically significant relationship between home language and post-graduation workforce outcomes in all three timeframes:

1 year after graduation: χ²(4) = 609.72, Cramer’s V = 0.100, p < .001, N = 61,485
5 years after graduation: χ²(4) = 227.55, Cramer’s V = 0.071, p < .001, N = 45,359
10 years after graduation: χ²(4) = 140.40, Cramer’s V = 0.074, p < .001, N = 25,464

The relationship is modest in magnitude and remains statistically significant across all three intervals.
Interpretation Over Time
1 Year After Graduation:
Home language is associated with differences in early post-graduation pathways, including access to meaningful regional employment. Graduates from non-English-speaking homes are engaged in meaningful regional employment at rates similar to those from English-speaking homes (13.9% vs. 13.0%). However, this similarity masks substantial differences in other outcomes. Graduates from non-English-speaking homes are far less likely to be attending postsecondary education (29.9% vs. 52.3%) and are much more likely to have no Minnesota employment record (29.0% vs. 12.8%) or to be in not-meaningful employment and not attending postsecondary education (22.3% vs. 18.9%). These patterns suggest that early regional employment among graduates from non-English-speaking households coexists with higher levels of workforce instability and disconnection.
5 Years After Graduation:
By five years after graduation, differences in meaningful regional employment become more pronounced. Graduates from English-speaking homes are substantially more likely to hold meaningful regional jobs (23.6% vs. 12.7%), while graduates from non-English-speaking homes are nearly twice as likely to have no Minnesota employment record (47.6% vs. 28.9%). Rates of meaningful employment elsewhere in Minnesota are also lower among non-English-home graduates. These differences indicate growing disparities in regional workforce attachment and employment stability over time.
10 Years After Graduation:
Ten years after graduation, disparities remain substantial. Graduates from non-English-speaking homes are far less likely to be engaged in meaningful regional employment (10.0% vs. 24.6%) and are much more likely to have no Minnesota employment record (65.9% vs. 43.5%). Although differences in not-meaningful employment narrow somewhat, meaningful employment—both regional and statewide—remains significantly less common among graduates from non-English-speaking households. At this stage, home language is closely associated with long-term regional retention and workforce stability.
Conclusion
Home language is associated with consistent differences in access to meaningful regional employment in Northwest Minnesota. While graduates from non-English-speaking homes initially enter the regional workforce at rates similar to their peers, they face substantially higher levels of employment instability and long-term detachment from the regional labor market. Over time, these differences widen, resulting in markedly lower rates of meaningful regional employment a decade after graduation.

title: Local Employment Cross-tabs
text:
english.learner

Key Findings
There is a statistically significant relationship between English learner status and post-graduation workforce outcomes in all three timeframes:

1 year after graduation: χ²(4) = 2203.62, Cramer’s V = 0.189, p < .001, N = 61,485
5 years after graduation: χ²(4) = 394.14, Cramer’s V = 0.093, p < .001, N = 45,359
10 years after graduation: χ²(4) = 100.40, Cramer’s V = 0.063, p < .001, N = 25,464

The relationship is moderate one year after graduation and weakens over time, though it remains statistically significant at each interval.
Interpretation Over Time
1 Year After Graduation:
English learner status is associated with pronounced differences in early post-graduation pathways, including access to meaningful regional employment. Graduates identified as English learners are slightly more likely to be engaged in meaningful regional employment (15.2% vs. 12.9%), indicating early workforce attachment. At the same time, English learners are far less likely to be attending postsecondary education (17.4% vs. 53.7%) and far more likely to have no Minnesota employment record (33.0% vs. 12.1%) or to be in not-meaningful employment and not attending postsecondary education (29.8% vs. 18.4%). These patterns indicate that early regional workforce attachment among English learners is often accompanied by elevated levels of instability and disconnection.
5 Years After Graduation:
Five years after graduation, English learners are less likely to hold meaningful regional employment (19.1% vs. 23.5%) and substantially more likely to have no Minnesota employment record (42.3% vs. 28.8%). They are also more likely to be in not-meaningful employment (24.4% vs. 18.1%). Differences in meaningful employment elsewhere in Minnesota persist, indicating continued disparities in access to stable employment both within and outside the region.
10 Years After Graduation:
Ten years after graduation, disparities remain evident. Former English learners are significantly less likely to be engaged in meaningful regional employment (15.8% vs. 24.5%) and more likely to have no Minnesota employment record (54.8% vs. 43.7%). They also continue to show higher rates of not-meaningful employment (22.4% vs. 16.7%). Although overall workforce participation stabilizes over time, English learner status remains associated with long-term differences in regional workforce attachment and employment quality.
Conclusion
English learner status is associated with sustained differences in workforce pathways in Northwest Minnesota. While English learners often enter the regional workforce earlier, they experience higher levels of employment instability and long-term detachment from meaningful regional employment. These differences persist well beyond high school, underscoring the importance of strengthening durable, high-quality regional employment pathways for English learner graduates.

title: Local Employment Cross-tabs
section: Summary of all high school characteristic variables
text:
Summary of all high school characteristic variables
Across the four high school characteristics examined—district rurality (Dem_Desc), Economic Development Region (EDR), local unemployment rates, and local wage levels during high school—there are statistically significant relationships with post-graduation workforce outcomes in Northwest Minnesota. While all four characteristics are associated with differences in workforce pathways, the strength, persistence, and nature of these relationships vary, particularly with respect to meaningful regional employment.
Relative Strength of Relationships
Among the high school characteristics examined, local unemployment conditions during high school show the clearest and most consistent differentiation across workforce outcomes. Although the magnitude of differences is modest, average unemployment rates are systematically higher among individuals who later engage in meaningful regional employment, with separation between outcome groups becoming more clearly defined at five and ten years after graduation.
District rurality (Dem_Desc) and Economic Development Region (EDR) show small but persistent relationships with workforce outcomes, particularly with respect to regional workforce attachment. Graduates from more rural districts and from school districts located within the Northwest EDR are consistently more likely to engage in meaningful employment within the region, while graduates from more urban districts or non-Northwest EDRs are more likely to work elsewhere in Minnesota or to have no Minnesota employment record. Although these differences are modest in size, they remain evident across all timeframes.
In contrast, local wage levels during high school show the smallest overall differentiation across workforce outcomes. While statistically significant differences are present, average wage context varies only slightly across outcome groups. Individuals in meaningful regional employment tend to be associated with marginally higher local wage contexts than those in not-meaningful employment or without Minnesota employment records, but these differences are limited in magnitude and diminish over time.
Cross-Cutting Patterns in Workforce Outcomes
Several consistent patterns emerge across high school characteristics. Graduates who later engage in meaningful regional employment are disproportionately associated with weaker local labor markets, characterized by higher unemployment rates and, to a lesser extent, rural or Northwest-based school districts. This suggests that regional workforce attachment is more common among individuals educated in contexts with fewer external labor market opportunities.
At the same time, stronger local economic contexts—particularly lower unemployment and higher wage levels—are associated with higher rates of postsecondary participation, meaningful employment elsewhere in Minnesota, or exit from Minnesota employment records. As a result, local economic advantage during high school does not necessarily translate into stronger long-term attachment to the Northwest regional workforce.
Across rurality and EDR, a consistent tension is evident: areas that retain graduates in the regional workforce also show higher concentrations of not-meaningful employment, indicating that retention and job quality do not always align. This pattern mirrors findings from the demographic analysis and underscores the importance of examining employment quality alongside regional attachment.
Bringing the High School Characteristics Together
Taken together, these findings indicate that high school context—particularly local labor market conditions and geographic location—is associated with how graduates connect to the workforce after high school. Differences across high school characteristics are generally small in magnitude, but they are consistent and persistent, especially for outcomes related to meaningful regional employment.
Graduates from rural and Northwest-based districts, and from areas with higher unemployment during high school, are more likely to remain attached to the regional workforce over time. However, this attachment is often accompanied by higher exposure to not-meaningful employment, highlighting the need for strategies that not only retain graduates locally but also expand access to stable, high-quality regional jobs. In contrast, graduates from stronger local labor markets are more likely to pursue opportunities outside the region, contributing to ongoing challenges in regional workforce retention.
Overall, the high school characteristics analysis reinforces the importance of understanding regional economic context when interpreting workforce outcomes, while also emphasizing that these contextual factors operate alongside—rather than overwhelming—individual demographic characteristics and educational pathways.

title: Local Employment Cross-tabs
text:
Dem_Desc

Key Findings
There is a statistically significant relationship between school district rurality (Dem_Desc) and post-graduation workforce outcomes at all three timeframes:

1 year after graduation: χ²(12) = 764.52, Cramer’s V = 0.064, p < .001, N = 61,485
5 years after graduation: χ²(12) = 1346.19, Cramer’s V = 0.099, p < .001, N = 45,359
10 years after graduation: χ²(12) = 822.21, Cramer’s V = 0.104, p < .001, N = 25,464

The strength of the relationship is modest one year after graduation and becomes stronger by five and ten years, indicating that differences by district rurality grow more pronounced over time.
Interpretation Over Time
1 Year After Graduation:
School district rurality is associated with differences in early post-graduation pathways, including access to meaningful regional employment. Graduates from entirely rural and town/rural mix districts are more likely to be engaged in meaningful employment within the region than graduates from entirely urban districts. In contrast, graduates from entirely urban districts are more likely to be attending postsecondary education, while rural and town/rural mix graduates are more likely to enter the workforce immediately. At the same time, graduates from urban districts show higher representation in no Minnesota employment record, while rural graduates are more likely to be concentrated in not-meaningful employment, indicating early regional attachment that does not always correspond to high-quality work.
5 Years After Graduation:
By five years after graduation, differences by district rurality become more clearly differentiated across outcome categories. Graduates from town/rural mix and entirely rural districts show the highest rates of meaningful regional employment, reflecting stronger long-term regional attachment. Graduates from entirely urban and urban/town/rural mix districts are more likely to hold meaningful employment elsewhere in Minnesota or to have no Minnesota employment record, suggesting greater geographic mobility or weaker attachment to the Northwest region. Differences in postsecondary attendance narrow substantially by this point, shifting disparities primarily toward employment location and quality.
10 Years After Graduation:
Ten years after graduation, district rurality remains strongly associated with workforce outcomes. Graduates from entirely rural and town/rural mix districts continue to show the highest rates of meaningful regional employment, indicating durable regional workforce anchoring. In contrast, graduates from entirely urban and urban/town/rural mix districts are more likely to be engaged in meaningful employment elsewhere in Minnesota or to have no Minnesota employment record. Rates of not-meaningful employment remain higher among graduates from more rural districts, suggesting that strong regional attachment can coexist with continued challenges in employment quality.
Conclusion
School district rurality is consistently associated with modest differences in regional workforce attachment in Northwest Minnesota.Graduates from more rural districts are more likely to enter and remain in meaningful regional employment, reflecting stronger long-term ties to the region. However, this attachment is accompanied by higher concentrations of not-meaningful employment, particularly in the early and mid-career years. Graduates from more urban districts are less likely to remain in the regional workforce over time, highlighting a persistent tension between regional retention and employment quality that varies systematically by high school context.

One year after graduatingFive years after graduatingTen years after graduating

title: Local Employment Cross-tabs
text:
EDR

Key Findings
There is a statistically significant relationship between the Economic Development Region (EDR) of the high school district and post-graduation workforce outcomes in all three timeframes:

1 year after graduation: χ²(8) = 448.34, Cramer’s V = 0.060, p < .001, N = 61,485
5 years after graduation: χ²(8) = 317.47, Cramer’s V = 0.059, p < .001, N = 45,359
10 years after graduation: χ²(8) = 195.80, Cramer’s V = 0.062, p < .001, N = 25,464

The relationship is modest but stable over time, indicating persistent differences in workforce outcomes by school district economic region.
Interpretation Over Time
1 Year After Graduation:
The Economic Development Region of the high school district is associated with differences in early post-graduation pathways, including access to meaningful regional employment. Graduates from school districts located within the Northwest EDR are more likely to be engaged in meaningful employment within the region, reflecting early regional workforce attachment. Graduates from districts in adjacent or non-Northwest EDRs are more likely to be attending postsecondary education or to have no Minnesota employment record, suggesting greater early mobility or delayed workforce entry. At the same time, Northwest EDR graduates show higher representation in not-meaningful employment, indicating that early regional attachment does not uniformly translate into high-quality work.
5 Years After Graduation:
By five years after graduation, regional differences persist. Graduates from Northwest EDR school districts continue to show higher rates of meaningful regional employment, reinforcing stronger long-term attachment to the regional labor market. Graduates from districts in other EDRs are more likely to hold meaningful employment elsewhere in Minnesota or to have no Minnesota employment record, suggesting sustained differences in geographic mobility and employment location. Postsecondary attendance plays a smaller role at this stage, with disparities increasingly concentrated in where graduates work rather than whether they are working.
10 Years After Graduation:
Ten years after graduation, the relationship between school district EDR and workforce outcomes remains evident. Graduates from Northwest EDR districts continue to exhibit the highest levels of meaningful regional employment, indicating durable regional anchoring. In contrast, graduates from districts in other EDRs are more likely to be employed outside the region or to lack a Minnesota employment record. Differences in not-meaningful employment remain, suggesting that while Northwest graduates are more likely to stay in the region, they also face ongoing challenges related to employment quality.
Conclusion
The Economic Development Region of the high school district is consistently associated with small but persistent differences in regional workforce attachment in Northwest Minnesota. Graduates from Northwest EDR districts are more likely to secure and maintain meaningful regional employment, reflecting strong local ties. However, this regional retention is accompanied by higher concentrations of not-meaningful employment, highlighting an ongoing tension between keeping graduates in the region and ensuring access to high-quality regional jobs. These findings underscore the importance of aligning regional economic development strategies with pathways to stable, meaningful employment for local graduates.

title: Local Employment Cross-tabs
text:
avg.unemp.rate

Key Findings
There is a statistically significant relationship between post-graduation workforce outcomes and the average unemployment rate of the local labor market during an individual’s high school years at all three timeframes:

1 year after graduation: F(4, 60,241) = 18.61, p < .001

5 years after graduation: F(4, 44,379) = 40.92, p < .001

10 years after graduation: F(3, 24,888) = 85.79, p < .001

Mean unemployment rates differ significantly across workforce outcome categories at each timepoint, with clearer separation between groups emerging over time.
Interpretation Over Time
1 Year After Graduation:
One year after graduation, workforce outcomes are associated with statistically significant but modest differences in the average unemployment rate of the local labor market experienced during high school. Individuals who later enter meaningful regional employment are associated with higher average local unemployment rates than those who later pursue postsecondary education, obtain meaningful employment elsewhere in Minnesota, or have no Minnesota employment record. Differences between meaningful regional employment and not-meaningful employment are smaller but still statistically significant. These results suggest that early entry into meaningful regional employment is more common among graduates from weaker local labor markets, though the magnitude of these differences is limited.
5 Years After Graduation:
By five years after graduation, differences across outcome groups become more pronounced, though they remain modest in size. Individuals engaged in meaningful regional employment continue to be associated with higher average unemployment rates during their high school years than individuals in all other outcome categories. Those with meaningful employment elsewhere in Minnesota and those who were attending postsecondary education are associated with lower-unemployment local contexts, while individuals in not-meaningful employment fall between these groups. Individuals with no Minnesota employment record are associated with the lowest average unemployment rates. These patterns indicate persistent differences in regional workforce attachment linked to local economic context, rather than large differences in labor market exposure.
10 Years After Graduation:
Ten years after graduation, the separation between outcome groups is more distinct, though still best characterized as systematic rather than large in magnitude. Individuals in meaningful regional employment are associated with higher average unemployment rates during their high school years than those in all other workforce outcome categories. The largest differences are observed between meaningful regional employment and no Minnesota employment record, followed by differences with meaningful employment elsewhere in Minnesota and not-meaningful employment. These results indicate that long-term regional workforce attachment is more common among graduates from persistently weaker local labor markets, while graduates from stronger labor markets are more likely to work outside the region or exit Minnesota employment records.
Conclusion
Average unemployment rates in the local labor market during high school are consistently associated with differences in post-graduation workforce pathways, particularly patterns of regional workforce attachment. Across all three timeframes, individuals engaged in meaningful regional employment are associated with higher-unemployment local contexts than individuals in other workforce outcome categories. While these differences are statistically robust and become more clearly differentiated over time, they are modest in magnitude and should be interpreted as reflecting systematic contextual differences, not strong or deterministic effects. These findings highlight the role of local economic context in shaping patterns of regional retention without overstating its influence on individual outcomes.

One year after graduationFive years after graduatingTen years after graduating



data <- meaningful.emp.northwest %>%
  select(grad.year.1, avg.unemp.rate) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023") %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  group_by(grad.year.1) %>%
  mutate(q1 = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, .25),
         q3 = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, .75),
         iqr = IQR(avg.unemp.rate),
         low.outlier = q1 - 1.5*iqr,
         high.outlier = q3 + 1.5*iqr) %>%
  filter(avg.unemp.rate > low.outlier,
         avg.unemp.rate < high.outlier) %>%
  ungroup()

labels <- data %>%
  group_by(grad.year.1) %>%
  summarize(Min = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, .0),
            Q1 = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, .25),
            Median = median(avg.unemp.rate),
            Q3 = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, .75),
            Max = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, 1),
            Mean = mean(avg.unemp.rate)) %>%
  ungroup()

plot <- ggplot(data, aes(grad.year.1, avg.unemp.rate, fill = grad.year.1, group = grad.year.1)) +
  geom_boxplot_interactive() +
  geom_label(data = labels, aes(x = grad.year.1, y = 15, label = paste("Min: ", comma(Min, accuracy = .1), "\nQ1: ", comma(Q1, accuracy = .1), "\nMedian: ", comma(Median, accuracy = .1), "\nQ3: ", comma(Q3, accuracy = .1), "\nMax: ", comma(Max, accuracy = ), "\nMean: ", comma(Mean, accuracy = .1))), color = "white") +
  labs(x="", y = "", color="", title = "Boxplots of average unemployment by employment state")+
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::comma,
                     limits = c(0, 20))+
  scale_fill_manual(values = brewer.pal(n = 6, "RdYlBu")) +
  theme_bar+
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        text = element_text(size = 18),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = .9))


girafe(ggobj = plot, width_svg = 10, height_svg = 10) %>%
  girafe_options(opts_selection(type = "none"),
                 opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE))

  filter(grad.year.5 != "After 2023") %>%
  drop_na() %>%

title: Local Employment Cross-tabs
text:
avg.wages.pct.state

Key Findings
There is a statistically significant relationship between post-graduation workforce outcomes and average county wages during an individual’s high school years, measured as a percentage of the statewide average wage, at all three timeframes:

1 year after graduation: F(4, 60,856) = 87.31, p < .001

5 years after graduation: F(4, 44,576) = 61.60, p < .001

10 years after graduation: F(3, 24,684) = 52.07, p < .001

Although mean differences in wage context are statistically significant across outcome groups, the magnitude of these differences is small, indicating modest differentiation by local wage conditions.
Interpretation Over Time
1 Year After Graduation:
One year after graduation, workforce outcomes are associated with small but statistically significant differences in average county wages during high school. Individuals who later enter meaningful regional employment are associated with slightly higher local wage contexts than those who later hold not-meaningful employment or have no Minnesota employment record. Differences between meaningful regional employment and meaningful employment elsewhere in Minnesota are minimal and only marginally significant, while individuals attending postsecondary education are associated with wage contexts similar to or slightly above those tied to meaningful regional employment. Overall, these results suggest that early regional employment is not concentrated among graduates from the lowest-wage local contexts, but wage differences across outcomes remain limited in size.
5 Years After Graduation:
By five years after graduation, small differences persist. Individuals engaged in meaningful regional employment continue to be associated with higher average local wages than those in not-meaningful employment or with no Minnesota employment record. In contrast, individuals holding meaningful employment elsewhere in Minnesota are associated with slightly higher wage contexts than those in meaningful regional employment. Differences between postsecondary attendance and not-meaningful employment narrow, reinforcing that wage context plays a limited but consistent role in differentiating workforce outcomes.
10 Years After Graduation:
Ten years after graduation, distinctions by wage context remain statistically significant but modest. Individuals in meaningful regional employment are associated with higher average county wages than those in not-meaningful employment or with no Minnesota employment record, while differences between meaningful regional employment and meaningful employment elsewhere in Minnesota are no longer statistically meaningful. These patterns suggest that local wage context during high school continues to differentiate stable employment outcomes from less stable or disconnected pathways, though the overall magnitude of wage differences remains small.
Conclusion
Average county wage levels during high school are consistently associated with modest differences in post-graduation workforce pathways. Across all three timeframes, individuals engaged in meaningful regional employment are associated with slightly higher local wage contexts than those in not-meaningful employment or without Minnesota employment records. However, these differences are small in magnitude and should be interpreted as reflecting incremental contextual variation, rather than strong separation across outcomes. Together with unemployment patterns, these findings suggest that local labor market conditions shape where and how graduates attach to the workforce, without exerting large or deterministic effects on individual outcomes.

One year after graduatingFive years after graduatingTen years after graduating
